text: Tomato is one of the most important food and economic vegetable crops worldwide, owing to its extensive cultivation, its diverse forms of consumption, and its use in a large number of food industries. It is not only consumed fresh, but is also used in the production of tomato paste, juices, sauces, ketchup, peeled and canned tomatoes, powders, concentrates, and other products. Tomato occupies a prominent position within agricultural systems because it is a relatively high-value crop, has a rapid economic turnover, and requires intensive labor and agricultural inputs. It is also linked to a broad chain of activities that includes seed and transplant production, fertilizers and pesticides, crop husbandry operations, harvesting, transport, grading, packing, marketing, and food processing. However, this major economic importance makes tomato highly sensitive to any decline in yield or quality. Exposure of the crop to a serious insect pest does not merely mean the loss of part of the foliage or fruit; it may also lead to a reduction in marketable yield, increased control costs, rejection of some consignments, a higher percentage of losses, and disruption of supply schedules to processors and markets. Among the pests that have caused a fundamental shift in tomato production systems worldwide during recent decades, Tuta absoluta, commonly known as the tomato leafminer or tomato borer, stands out as one of the most serious invasive pests threatening tomato production in open fields and protected cultivation.

Global Production Volume Tomato is cultivated in most agricultural regions worldwide, from temperate and Mediterranean areas to tropical and subtropical regions. According to statistics from the Food and Agriculture Organization, global tomato production reached approximately 192 million tonnes in 2023, making tomato the leading vegetable crop in terms of global production volume in that year. [1] This enormous production volume reflects the importance of tomato to global food security and also demonstrates the sensitivity of markets to major disruptions affecting key production regions, whether due to pests, diseases, heat waves, water scarcity, or supply-chain disturbances. The importance of tomato is not limited to countries that produce it for domestic consumption. Some countries depend on it as: A crop for fresh export. A major processing crop. A source of agricultural and industrial employment. An important component of food security. A raw material for tomato paste and sauce factories. A strategic crop in regional and international trade. Nutritional Value Tomato fruit contains a range of important nutritional and health-promoting compounds, including: Vitamin C. Potassium. Organic acids. Carotenoids. Lycopene. A number of phenolic compounds and antioxidants. Lycopene is one of the principal compounds associated with the red color of the fruit and is also an important quality indicator in tomato cultivars intended for processing. Its value and concentration are affected by cultivar, ripening stage, temperature, nutrition, and the health status of the plant and fruit. Tomato quality is not limited to nutritional value alone; it also includes color, firmness, size, shape, total soluble solids, acidity, and freedom of the fruit from insect and disease damage. Processing Importance Production objectives for processing tomatoes differ from those for tomatoes intended for fresh consumption. Processors place greater emphasis on traits such as: High total soluble solids and °Brix. Strong and uniform red color. High lycopene content. Low defect incidence. Fruit firmness and tolerance to transport. Ease of harvesting. A high proportion of fruit ripening at the same time. Low losses during grading and processing. Insect infestation directly affects these characteristics. An infested fruit may be rejected even when its size and color are suitable, because the presence of mines, holes, or larvae inside the fruit makes it commercially and industrially unacceptable. Insect injury also opens entry sites for secondary decay organisms, thereby increasing the proportion of damaged fruit, raising grading costs, and reducing the amount of raw material suitable for processing. Social and Economic Importance The value of the tomato crop extends beyond the sale price of the fruit, as it is associated with large numbers of workers in: Nurseries. Seed production. Crop husbandry. Irrigation and fertigation systems. Pest management. Harvesting. Packing. Transport. Wholesale and retail markets. Food-processing plants. Quality-control and pesticide-residue laboratories. Consequently, any major crisis in tomato production transmits its effects from the field to the remaining links of the value chain. Farmers' income may decline, some labor activities may stop, processing-plant operating efficiency may decrease, consumer prices may rise, or the need to import processed products may increase.

sectionTitle: The Importance of Tomato in Egypt
text: The Position of Tomato in Egyptian Agriculture Tomato is one of the most important vegetable crops in Egypt in terms of cultivated area, production, and consumption. Climatic conditions and the diversity of production regions allow it to be grown across multiple planting seasons and dates, making the crop available in markets for extended periods of the year. Tomato production is distributed across many regions, including: The Nile Delta governorates. The Nile Valley. Old agricultural lands. Nubaria. Wadi El Natrun. The northwestern coast. Western Minya areas. Beni Suef and Fayoum. Upper Egypt. New and reclaimed lands. A previous assessment prepared by the Food and Agriculture Organization on the tomato value chain in Egypt showed that Egyptian production reached approximately 7.9 million tonnes in 2016, placing Egypt at that time fifth among tomato-producing countries worldwide. This figure is cited here as a historical indicator of the size of the sector, not as a fixed value for every season. [2] Multiple Planting Seasons and Production Regions The multiplicity of tomato planting seasons in Egypt helps maintain supply to markets and processing plants, but it simultaneously creates a major challenge for pest management. When tomato fields of different ages are located close to one another, pests can move from older fields into newly planted crops. Abandoned fields, crop residues, volunteer plants, or wild solanaceous hosts may become continuous sources for pest reproduction and spread. This phenomenon is sometimes known as the Green Bridge, in which suitable host plants remain available to the pest for prolonged periods, preventing a complete break in its life cycle between seasons. The problem becomes more evident when a grower stops controlling a severely infested field before removing, shredding, and ploughing under the plants, because large numbers of adult moths then migrate into younger neighboring fields. Fresh-Market and Processing Tomatoes Tomatoes are produced in Egypt for two principal purposes: Production for the fresh market: This system focuses on appearance, size, color, firmness, tolerance to handling, and freedom of the fruit from visible defects. Production for processing: This system focuses on °Brix, color, concentration, firmness, uniform ripening, low defect incidence, and conversion efficiency into paste or concentrates. In both systems, Tuta absoluta infestation represents a direct threat. In the fresh market, holes, mines, and deformities reduce the market grade of the fruit. In processing tomatoes, infestation increases the proportion of fruit rejected during grading, raises losses, and may lead to rejection of loads that exceed acceptable limits for defects and infestation. The Impact of Losses Along the Value Chain Tomato is naturally relatively perishable and is highly affected by poor handling, delayed transport, high temperature, and mechanical and insect damage. Therefore, crop loss is not limited to fruit that falls in the field; it also includes: Infested fruit that cannot be sold. Fruit rejected at grading stations. Fruit rejected by the processing plant. Losses during transport. Fruit decayed as a result of insect wounds. Price reduction due to poor quality. Loss of the added value that could have been achieved through processing. The Food and Agriculture Organization has conducted specific studies on losses in the tomato value chain in Egypt, reflecting the economic importance of reducing losses from the field to the consumer. [2]

sectionTitle: Why Are Pests an Economic Threat?
text: The Difference Between Pest Presence and Economic Damage The presence of a single insect or a limited number of insects does not necessarily mean that an economic loss has occurred or that chemical intervention is required. An economic decision should be linked to pest density, the life stage present, crop age, the proportion of infested plants or fruit, crop value, intervention cost, and the damage expected if control is not undertaken. Pest management science distinguishes among three principal concepts: Infestation: The presence of the pest or its life stages on the plant or within its tissues. Damage: The actual effect of the pest on plant tissues, such as mining, feeding, or fruit injury. Economic Loss: The financial reduction resulting from yield loss, quality deterioration, or increased production costs. A plant may tolerate limited infestation, whereas a small infestation occurring at a sensitive stage, such as the beginning of fruit set or fruit filling, may cause a much greater loss. Direct Yield Loss Pests feed on different plant parts and may cause: Seedling death. Reduction in leaf area. Reduced photosynthesis. Damage to growing points. Flower desiccation. Fruit-set drop. Fruit infestation. Fruit decay. Reduction in total yield. Reduction in marketable yield. In the case of Tuta absoluta, damage is not limited to the leaf. Larvae can attack leaves, stems, growing points, and fruit, causing effects that extend simultaneously to vegetative growth, yield, and quality. Reduced Market Quality A field may produce a high total fruit weight, yet a large proportion may be unmarketable because of infestation. This point is extremely important because the true economic criterion is not always total yield, but rather: > Marketable yield or yield accepted for processing. Perforated fruit, fruit containing larvae, or fruit affected by decay may be included when total field weight is estimated, but they do not represent actual income to the grower. In fresh-tomato markets, a small hole in the fruit can greatly reduce its commercial value. In processing plants, infested fruit may increase the percentage of defects and losses during grading, and may also raise the microbial load and affect the safety and quality of the raw material. Increased Production Costs Pests add multiple cost items to production per feddan, including: Cost of pesticides. Cost of spraying and operation. Labor costs. Purchase of traps and pheromones. Monitoring and inspection costs. Cost of biological-control agents. Increased number of field visits. Cost of removing severely infested plants. Cost of crop-residue disposal. Cost of residue analysis. Cost of respraying when a treatment fails. When random spraying is relied upon, the grower may incur high costs without achieving effective pest control, particularly when the pesticide is applied at the wrong time or against a life stage concealed within plant tissue. Pesticide Residues and Preharvest Intervals Excessive or unregulated pesticide use may lead to: Exceeding maximum residue limits. Failure to comply with the preharvest interval. Rejection of domestic or export consignments. Difficulty marketing the crop to processors committed to quality specifications. Increased cost of residue testing. Restrictions on the use of certain active ingredients. Damage to the reputation of the farm or supplying company. This problem is particularly sensitive in tomato because of repeated picking in fresh-market cultivars or the proximity of once-over harvest in processing tomatoes. Development of Pesticide Resistance Resistance develops when individuals that are more tolerant to a pesticide survive and reproduce, after which the frequency of resistance traits within the pest population increases over successive generations. The risk of resistance increases under the following conditions: Repeated use of the same active ingredient. Use of products with different trade names that belong to the same mode of action. Use of doses below those recommended. Repeated spraying without monitoring. Incomplete spray coverage. Complete reliance on chemical control. A high number of pest generations during the season. Studies have shown that Tuta absoluta is capable of developing resistance to different insecticide groups through changes in target-site sensitivity or increased ability to detoxify the pesticide. [3] Mortality of Natural Enemies Broad-spectrum pesticides may kill parasitoids and predators that occur naturally in the field. When natural-enemy populations decline, the agroecosystem loses part of its natural capacity to regulate pest populations. In some cases, the pest population declines for a short period after spraying, then rises rapidly again because of: Survival of some resistant individuals. Mortality of natural enemies. Immigration of new moths from neighboring fields. Continued presence of eggs and larvae inside mines. Failure to eliminate infestation sources in old fields. Therefore, the success of a spray treatment should not be evaluated only by the number of insects killed immediately, but by the sustained reduction in pest population and the preservation of biological balance within the field. Economic-Loss Equation The economic effect of a pest can be expressed in a simplified form by the following relationship: Total economic impact of the pest = Loss of marketable yield Reduction in price due to lower quality Control costs Grading and rejection costs Supply or export losses Effects of pesticide resistance Indirect environmental and health impacts. It is therefore clear that the most serious pest is not necessarily the one that consumes the greatest amount of plant tissue, but rather the pest that combines rapid reproduction, difficulty of detection, high control costs, attack on the marketable part of the plant, and the capacity to develop resistance.

sectionTitle: Introduction to the Threat Posed by Tuta absoluta
text: What Is Tuta absoluta? Tuta absoluta is a small lepidopteran insect native to South America that, within a relatively short period, became a widespread invasive pest threatening tomato production in Europe, Africa, Asia, and the Middle East. It is known worldwide by names such as: Tomato leaf miner. Tomato borer. South American tomato moth. South American tomato pinworm. The name tomato leafminer is widely used, but it does not alone describe all forms of damage because the larvae do not attack leaves only; they can also attack stems, growing points, flowers, and fruit. The larval stage is responsible for direct damage, as the larva mines into plant tissues and feeds in a site that is relatively protected from external conditions and from many surface-applied spray treatments. Why Is It Considered One of the Most Serious Tomato Pests? The seriousness of Tuta absoluta results from the combination of several biological and behavioral traits in a single pest. First: Concealed Feeding The larva spends a large part of its active period inside leaf, stem, or fruit tissue. Consequently, spray droplets may reach the plant surface without reaching the concealed larva at an effective concentration. The presence of larvae inside mines also makes early detection of infestation more difficult unless plants are inspected regularly and carefully. Second: High Reproductive Capacity The insect can produce successive generations throughout the year when suitable temperature and host plants are available. Its relatively short life cycle under warm conditions leads to a rapid increase in population if infestation is not detected early. Third: Attack on Different Crop Stages Infestation can begin at the seedling stage and continue through vegetative growth, flowering, fruit set, fruit filling, and until near harvest. The economic impact varies according to the timing of infestation: Early infestation may weaken establishment and growth. Infestation of growing points may deform plant growth. Leaf infestation reduces effective leaf area. Fruit infestation causes direct loss of the marketable portion. Fourth: Attack on Multiple Plant Parts Larvae can attack: Leaves. Petioles. Growing points. Tender stems. Calyces. Green fruit. Fruit that has begun to color. Ripe fruit. This diversity of infestation sites makes control more difficult and means that monitoring based on traps alone is insufficient. Fifth: High Potential Losses Monitoring protocols and plant-protection reports indicate that severe unmanaged infestation can cause losses ranging from 80 to 100% under some conditions, in both protected cultivation and open fields. [4] However, this figure must be interpreted accurately. It represents the potential upper limit under severe outbreaks and in the absence of effective control; it does not mean that every infested field will automatically lose this proportion. Actual losses depend on: Timing of initial infestation. Pest density. Plant age. Cultivar. Canopy condition. Temperature. Presence of neighboring infestation sources. Monitoring efficiency. Speed of intervention. Type of control program used. Sixth: Rapid Spread The movement of fruit, transplants, packing materials, and agricultural trade, together with the insect's capacity for local dispersal, facilitated the spread of Tuta absoluta from its native range to extensive areas. After it was recorded in Spain in 2006, it spread rapidly across the Mediterranean Basin, Africa, and Asia and became a major threat to tomato production in most regions where it became established. [5] Seventh: Capacity to Develop Resistance Tuta absoluta has a short life cycle and high reproductive capacity, two factors that increase the probability of selecting resistant individuals when insecticides with the same mode of action are repeatedly used. Resistance to multiple insecticide groups has been recorded in different pest populations, making rotation among IRAC groups, reduction of chemical selection pressure, and integration of nonchemical control methods essential rather than optional. [3] Eighth: Movement Between Fields Adult moths may move from old, neglected fields or fields where control has stopped into younger neighboring fields. The risk of this migration increases when: Infested plants are left after harvest has ended. Shredding and ploughing are delayed. Planting seasons overlap. Alternative hosts or volunteer plants are present. Traps are not used in production areas. Each grower acts independently without coordination with neighboring growers. Consequently, Tuta absoluta management cannot be limited to field boundaries; it requires area-wide management at the level of the agricultural production zone and the grower community.

Egyptian conditions provide several factors that may favor continued pest activity, including warm weather during large parts of the year, multiple tomato planting seasons, overlapping crops, the presence of potato, eggplant, and other solanaceous hosts, and the retention of crop residues in some areas. Movement of the pest from one planting season to another may maintain insect pressure throughout the season. Therefore, each field should be viewed as part of a wider production system that includes: The nursery. Neighboring fields. Fields in which harvesting has ended. Solanaceous crops. Weeds and wild hosts. Packing stations. Crates used to transport fruit and transplants. Grading and processing waste. In open-field processing tomatoes, infestation may appear early and then increase as the canopy expands and plants overlap. If the canopy deteriorates late in the season, fruit becomes more exposed to heat and secondary infections, while larvae continue to attack fruit close to harvest. Fields with delayed ripening may also be exposed to immigration of moths from early fields where harvesting has ended or where control programs have stopped.

sectionTitle: Why Is Chemical Control Alone Insufficient?
text: Global experience has demonstrated that reliance on pesticides alone does not achieve sustainable control of Tuta absoluta. The insect is protected inside mines during part of its life, and eggs, larvae, pupae, and moths may all be present in the field at the same time. Repeated spraying may also lead to: Selection of resistant strains. Mortality of natural enemies. Increased pesticide residues. Higher production costs. Failure of some active ingredients. Fewer available options close to harvest. Disruption of biological balance in the field. Accordingly, recent scientific reviews emphasize that effective management of Tuta absoluta must be based on Integrated Pest Management (IPM) by combining monitoring, traps, cultural practices, biological control, and selective pesticides within a single program. [5, 6] Applying integrated pest management does not mean completely refraining from pesticide use. Rather, it means using a pesticide when needed, at the correct timing, with the appropriate active ingredient, and after assessing pest density, while preserving natural enemies as far as possible and preventing the development of resistance.

sectionTitle: Initial Principles for Successful Pest Management
text: Before addressing the practical details in the following chapters, the general foundations for successful control can be summarized as follows: 1. Prevent introduction of the pest through infested transplants. 2. Install pheromone traps early, before severe infestation appears. 3. Inspect plants in the field and do not rely on traps alone. 4. Remove heavily infested leaves or plants at the beginning of an outbreak when feasible. 5. Rapidly dispose of crop residues after harvest is completed. 6. Prevent neglected fields from remaining a source of moths. 7. Control weeds and alternative hosts. 8. Protect naturally occurring natural enemies. 9. Use biological and microbial control agents within a clearly defined program. 10. Select pesticides according to the active ingredient and mode of action, not the trade name alone. 11. Rotate pesticide groups according to the IRAC classification. 12. Comply with recommended doses and preharvest intervals. 13. Evaluate results according to infestation level and damaged fruit, not according to the number of sprays. 14. Coordinate control among neighboring fields as far as possible.

Tuta absoluta is not merely an insect that attacks tomato leaves; it is a complex economic pest that affects production, quality, control costs, crop safety, and the sustainability of pesticide programs. Its seriousness arises from its capacity to: Remain concealed within plant tissues. Produce multiple generations. Attack leaves, stems, and fruit. Spread rapidly between fields and regions. Survive on multiple plant hosts. Develop pesticide resistance. Cause severe losses when detection is delayed. Successful management therefore begins before economic damage appears, through prevention, monitoring, and early detection, followed by the use of an integrated set of cultural, biological, behavioral, and chemical measures. The following chapters will address the pest's definition, names, classification, and global distribution, followed by a description of its life stages, life cycle, hosts, and infestation symptoms, before proceeding to a detailed and comprehensive chapter on integrated management programs for Tuta absoluta in open fields. References

Tuta absoluta is considered one of the most serious insect pests attacking tomato crops, both in open fields and protected cultivation. It has gained this status because of its ability to infest multiple plant parts, its rapid reproduction, the concealed feeding habit of its larvae within plant tissues, the ease with which adult moths move between fields, and its capacity to develop resistance to several insecticides. The severity of Tuta absoluta is not measured solely by the number of insects present in the field, but rather by its ability to cause direct damage within leaves, stems, growing points, and fruits. During the early stages of infestation, the plant may appear externally to be in acceptable condition, while larvae may already have begun forming mines within its tissues. Therefore, the nature of the insect, the damaging stage, and the mechanism of infestation must be understood before establishing any monitoring or control program.

Tuta absoluta is a small moth belonging to the order Lepidoptera, which includes butterflies and moths. It belongs to the small-sized family Gelechiidae, which includes many species whose larvae are characterized by boring or feeding within plant tissues. The commonly used scientific name is: Tuta absoluta (Meyrick) Some modern taxonomic references also use the name: Phthorimaea absoluta (Meyrick) The difference in genus name among some sources does not mean that two different pests are being discussed; rather, it results from taxonomic revisions concerning the relationships among the genera within this group. The name Tuta absoluta remains the most widely used name in agricultural guides, control programs, pheromone traps, and agricultural trade. The insect undergoes complete metamorphosis, meaning that it passes through four main stages: 1. Egg. 2. Larva. 3. Pupa. 4. Adult insect or moth. Each stage performs a different function within the life cycle. The egg represents the beginning of the generation, the larva is the feeding, growth, and damaging stage, the pupa is the transformation stage into the adult insect, while the moths are responsible for mating, dispersal, and egg laying. Tomato is the principal economic host of the pest, although it can infest other plants, particularly some species belonging to the family Solanaceae.

sectionTitle: Is Tuta absoluta a Moth, a Miner, or a Worm?
text: These terms describe different aspects of the insect: Moth: the winged adult insect. Larva or caterpillar: the stage that emerges from the egg and feeds on the plant. Miner: a description of the larva’s feeding behavior within plant tissues. Tomato leaf miner: a common name for the pest, but it does not reflect all forms of damage it causes. Tomato borer or tomato fruit borer: refers to its ability to penetrate fruits and stems. Weevil: a colloquial name used by some growers, but it is taxonomically inaccurate because the insect is not a true weevil belonging to the order Coleoptera. Therefore, Tuta absoluta is not a “worm” throughout its life. It is a moth whose larval stage is worm-like, and this is the stage that mines within the plant and causes damage.

Infestation usually begins when the female lays eggs on plant parts, after which the egg hatches and a small larva emerges. Following hatching, the larva searches for a suitable penetration point and rapidly enters the leaf, stem, bud, or fruit. Once the larva settles within plant tissue, it begins feeding and forms a mine or mined blotch. The appearance of the damage varies according to the infested plant part and larval age. Tuta absoluta is a pest that: Mines leaves. Mines stems and petioles. Attacks growing points. Bores into fruits. Can cause both direct and indirect damage. Can infest the plant from the nursery stage until close to harvest. For this reason, describing it solely as a “leaf miner” is incomplete, because the most severe economic losses are often associated with infestation of growing points, stems, and fruits.

sectionTitle: Nature of the Damage Caused by the Pest
text: The damage caused by Tuta absoluta can be divided into five interrelated types: 1. Direct feeding damage. 2. Physiological damage. 3. Structural damage. 4. Indirect damage resulting from secondary infection. 5. Economic and marketability damage. Direct Feeding Damage Direct damage occurs when larvae feed on plant tissues. In leaves, the larva consumes the mesophyll tissue located between the two epidermal layers, while the two outer epidermal layers may remain temporarily intact. This results in the formation of pale or translucent areas known as: Mines or mined blotches — Mines As feeding continues and the mine expands, the infested area gradually turns brown, then dries out and dies. In stems, petioles, and growing points, larvae feed within the tissues and form tunnels that may affect the movement of water and nutrients and the continued growth of young plant parts. In fruits, the larva enters beneath the epidermis or into the fleshy tissues, forming tunnels that are usually associated with feeding residues and frass. Physiological Damage The effect of leaf infestation is not limited to the presence of dry blotches; it also results in a reduction in the effective leaf area capable of carrying out photosynthesis. When infestation is severe, it may lead to: Reduced sugar production within the plant. Poor fruit nourishment. Reduced vegetative growth. Declining ability of the plant to fill fruits. Reduced final yield. Fruit exposure to sunlight due to canopy collapse. Increased risk of sunscald and elevated fruit temperature. Reduced plant capacity to withstand heat and water stress. The severity of this effect depends on the proportion of infested leaves, the location of infestation, plant age, timing of damage, and the extent to which the canopy continues to produce new leaves. Not every leaf mine represents the same level of loss. Infestation of an old lower leaf is not equivalent to infestation of a group of young leaves or the growing point during fruit set and fruit-filling stages. Structural Damage to Stems and Growing Points Larvae may bore into: Leaf petioles. Young shoots. Buds. Growing points. Tender stems. The calyx–fruit attachment zone. Infestation of the growing point in young plants can produce consequences more serious than the loss of part of a leaf, because it may cause: Arrest of main-stem growth. Death of the growing point. Emergence of irregular lateral shoots. Delayed plant growth. Disruption of the balance between vegetative and reproductive growth. Poor formation of early fruit clusters. Loss of part of the early yield. Tunnels within the stem may also weaken the tissue and cause wilting of the plant part beyond the infested zone, particularly when more than one larva is present or when infestation overlaps with plant pathogens. Direct Damage to Fruits Fruit infestation is the most economically serious form of damage because, in this case, the pest directly reaches the harvested part that is sold or delivered to the processing plant. Larvae may enter fruits: Near the calyx and pedicel. At points of contact between fruits and leaves. Through the sides of the fruit. Through cracks or weak areas in the epidermis. Sometimes through the lower part of the fruit or from areas that are not externally obvious. Infested fruits may show signs such as: Small entry holes. Exit holes. Dark frass near the opening. Superficial tunnels beneath the epidermis. Soft or sunken areas. Discoloration of tissues surrounding the tunnel. Presence of the larva or its shed skin inside the fruit. Development of rots around the infestation site. The entry hole may be extremely small compared with the extent of internal damage. Therefore, a rapid external inspection is not sufficient to determine fruit soundness, particularly when evaluating produce intended for processing or export. Indirect Damage and Secondary Infection The tunnels, holes, and wounds produced by larvae provide entry points for pathogens, especially fungi and bacteria that cause rots. Thus, the problem can develop from limited insect infestation into complex deterioration involving: 1. A wound caused by larval entry. 2. Tunnels within the tissues. 3. Accumulation of moisture and frass inside the tunnel. 4. Entry of pathogenic microorganisms. 5. Tissue breakdown and decay. 6. Complete loss of the fruit. Rots may continue to develop during harvesting, transport, and temporary storage. Consequently, losses may continue after the crop has left the field. This problem becomes more severe under warm and humid conditions, when fruits are piled, transport is delayed, and additional mechanical injuries are present. Economic and Marketability Damage Infestation does not have to cause fruit drop or complete fruit loss before an economic loss is recorded. The fruit may remain on the plant and its weight may be included in total yield, yet it may be unsuitable for marketing. Forms of economic loss include: Rejection of infested fruits in the fresh market. Downgrading of market grade. Increased sorting losses. Higher labor costs. Rejection of entire loads when infestation levels are high. Increased losses at the processing plant. Higher microbial load in the raw material. Reduced quantity of sound fruit suitable for processing. Increased number of sprays and higher control costs. Difficulty complying with preharvest intervals. Increased risk of pesticide residues. Reduced profits for both growers and processors. Therefore, the field should be evaluated using marketable yield, not total fruit weight alone.

The Larva Is the Stage Responsible for Damage The larval stage is the damaging stage in the life cycle of Tuta absoluta. The larva passes through several instars or molts, and the amount of tissue it consumes increases as it develops. Newly hatched larvae begin by forming small mines, which expand as they grow. The larva may move from one mine to another or from a leaf to another plant part. All larval instars are capable of causing damage, but the nature of the damage differs among them: Newly Hatched Larvae They are characterized by being: Extremely small. Rapid in searching for a penetration site. Able to initiate tiny mines that are difficult to detect. Potentially more susceptible to control during the short period between hatching and tissue penetration. Important targets for biological insecticides and products acting through contact or ingestion. Intermediate and Advanced Larval Instars They are characterized by: Consuming a larger quantity of tissue. Forming wider mines. Producing more visible frass. Being able to attack stems and fruits. Causing most of the visible damage. Being better protected within plant tissues. Potentially leaving one mine and moving to a new site. Therefore, control should not be delayed until large larvae and wide mines become visible, because this means that a substantial proportion of the damage has already occurred. Why Is the Egg Not Considered a Damaging Stage? The egg does not feed on the plant and therefore causes no direct damage. However, it is an important early indicator of the presence of a new generation before damage begins. Egg monitoring is useful because controlling or parasitizing eggs before hatching prevents larval emergence altogether. This explains the importance of egg parasitoids such as Trichogramma species and of inspecting young leaves before mines appear. The egg is not a damaging stage, but it is a critical stage for prevention. Why Is the Pupa Not Considered a Damaging Stage? The pupa is a non-feeding stage during which the larva transforms into an adult moth. It therefore causes no direct loss of plant tissue. However, the presence of pupae in the soil, leaves, or crop residues means that a new generation of moths will emerge after a period of time. For this reason, residue management, tillage, and removal of infested plants are important components in reducing pest sources. Why Is the Adult Moth Not Considered the Direct Damaging Stage? Adult moths, if they feed at all, feed on fluids and do not bore into tomato tissues or produce the mines found in leaves and fruits. Therefore, the moth does not cause the visible damage directly, but it has major epidemiological importance because it is responsible for: Mating. Egg laying. Movement between plants. Migration between fields. Reinfestation of newly planted crops. Spread of the pest over new distances. Accordingly, the moth can be described as the dispersal and reproductive stage, whereas the larva is the damaging stage. This distinction is essential when interpreting pheromone-trap catches. The trap measures adult male activity and does not directly count the larvae present inside the plant.

The infestation sequence can be simplified as follows: Stage One: Arrival of Moths in the Field Moths may enter from: A nearby infested field. Residues of an old crop. Infested transplants. Other solanaceous hosts. Volunteer plants or solanaceous weeds. Contaminated boxes or transport equipment in some cases. Stage Two: Egg Laying Females lay eggs on suitable plant parts, especially young tissues and leaves. Eggs may also be found near stems, the calyx, and fruits. Stage Three: Hatching A very small larva emerges from the egg and begins searching for a penetration site. Stage Four: Tissue Penetration The larva mines into the leaf, stem, or fruit and, after entering, becomes less exposed to external influences. Stage Five: Mine Expansion The larva continues feeding and growing, the damaged area expands, and frass accumulates within the mine or around openings. Stage Six: Movement The larva may leave the mine and move to another leaf, stem, or fruit. Therefore, the presence of an empty mine does not mean that infestation has ended. Stage Seven: Pupation and Emergence of a New Generation After completing its growth, the larva transforms into a pupa, from which a new moth emerges, lays eggs, and repeats the infestation cycle. Overlapping generations result in eggs, larvae, pupae, and moths being present in the field at the same time. This complicates control and prevents reliance on a single treatment.

sectionTitle: Why Is It Difficult for an Insecticide to Reach the Larva?
text: After the larva penetrates the tissue, it becomes partially surrounded by the layers of the leaf, stem, or fruit. Therefore, the spray solution may cover the external plant surface while failing to reach the larva in an effective quantity. Control becomes more difficult when: The larva is at an advanced instar. It is located deep within the fruit. The canopy is dense. Spray coverage is poor. The spray-water volume is insufficient. The spray does not reach both the upper and lower leaf surfaces. The product used is unsuitable for the target stage. Spraying is delayed until mines have expanded. Resistance to the active ingredient is present. For this reason, timing of intervention is extremely important. It is preferable to target the period immediately before or after hatching, before most larvae become established within plant tissues. However, this timing cannot be determined from traps alone. Trap data must be integrated with inspection of eggs, recent mines, larvae, and infested plants.

A Successful Invasive Pest Tuta absoluta possesses several characteristics that have enabled it to develop from a local pest in South America into a widely distributed international pest, including: Rapid reproduction. Multiple generations. Small body size. Ease of concealment. Ability to move with plant products. Capacity for flight and local dispersal. Presence of alternative hosts. Adaptation to a wide range of environmental conditions. Rapid colonization of new production areas. The pest exerts particularly high pressure in areas of continuous or overlapping tomato cultivation, where there is no sufficiently long host-free period. Crop Infestation at All Growth Stages Infestation may begin in the nursery and continue until the end of harvest. Consequently, the form of loss varies according to crop stage: | Crop Stage | Potential Effect of Infestation | | ----------- | ------------------------------- | | Nursery | Weak transplants and transfer of the pest to the field | | After transplanting | Slow establishment and infestation of growing points | | Vegetative growth | Reduction in leaf area | | Flowering and fruit set | Disruption of growth and infestation of young tissues | | Fruit filling | Reduced photosynthesis and fruit infestation | | Coloring and harvest | Fruit rejection, increased losses, and preharvest-interval problems | This extended period means that pest management must be a continuous program, not a curative spray applied only after damage appears. Overlapping Generations Under favorable conditions, the field may contain at the same time: Newly laid eggs. Small larvae. Large larvae. Pupae. Adult moths. A particular treatment may therefore affect one stage while the remaining stages survive and rebuild the population after a short period. Differences in plant age and multiple planting dates within the production area also allow the pest to continue moving from one field to another. Capacity to Cause Severe Losses In severe outbreaks that are not detected early and are not managed effectively, a very large proportion of the crop may be damaged, and the loss of marketable yield may approach complete crop loss in extreme cases. However, the estimate of “100% losses” should not be applied generally to every infestation. Actual losses vary according to several factors, including: Timing of initial infestation. Density of eggs and larvae. Percentage of infested fruits. Crop age. Cultivar. Temperature. Quality of monitoring. Speed of intervention. Condition of neighboring fields. Level of natural enemies. Effectiveness of the control program. Early presence of the pest does not inevitably mean crop loss, but it requires a rapid response based on monitoring. Capacity to Develop Resistance In many regions, populations of Tuta absoluta are exposed to strong selection pressure due to repeated insecticide use. With multiple generations and large numbers of individuals, the probability increases that some individuals capable of tolerating a particular active ingredient will survive. These individuals then reproduce, reducing the susceptibility of the pest population to that insecticide. Resistance increases when: The same active ingredient is repeatedly used. The same IRAC group is repeatedly used. Different trade names belonging to the same group are repeatedly used. The dose is reduced without a technical basis. Spraying is conducted at an unsuitable time. Control relies on insecticides alone. Migration continues from fields using different spray programs. A large number of treatments is applied during the season. Insecticide resistance is one of the principal reasons why Tuta absoluta remains a difficult and persistently serious pest. Movement from Old Fields to Newly Planted Fields A field that has completed harvesting may become a major source of moths if infested plants are left without shredding or plowing. A dangerous scenario occurs when: 1. Infestation increases in an old field. 2. Harvest approaches completion. 3. The grower stops spraying. 4. Infested plants remain in the field. 5. New moths emerge in large numbers. 6. They move to younger fields. 7. Infestation begins in newly planted crops. Therefore, crop-residue management is not a cosmetic practice, but a direct method of breaking the pest cycle and protecting neighboring fields. Low Acceptance Threshold for Infested Fruits The plant may tolerate a limited amount of leaf infestation, but an infested fruit is usually not commercially acceptable. A single hole may result in: Rejection of the fruit. Downgrading. Entry of rot organisms. Rejection during sorting. An increased percentage of defects in the load. Therefore, the economic threshold for fruit infestation is much lower than the threshold associated with the presence of a limited number of leaf mines. In processing tomatoes, it is necessary to distinguish between: Total yield. Red-fruit yield. Sound yield. Yield accepted by the processing plant. Percentage of infested, rotten, or rejected fruits.

sectionTitle: Difference Between Active and Old Infestation
text: Seeing a mine on a leaf does not necessarily mean that a live larva is still present inside it at the time of inspection. The larva may have: Remained within the mine. Left and moved to another leaf. Died. Been parasitized. Completed its development and left to pupate. Therefore, several mines should be opened and examined to determine whether infestation is active. Indicators of Active Infestation Presence of a live larva. Presence of fresh frass. Recently chewed tissue. Continued expansion of the mine. Presence of small, recent mines. Presence of newly laid eggs near growing tissues. Indicators of Old Infestation A completely dry mine. Brown, dead tissue. Absence of the larva. Presence of an exit hole. No further expansion of the mine. Confusing old and active infestation leads to inaccurate spray decisions. Insecticides may be applied because of old damage that contains no live larvae, or the field may be neglected because adult moth catches are low despite the presence of active larvae inside plants.

sectionTitle: Indicators That Should Be Monitored to Assess Risk
text: Tuta absoluta should not be evaluated using a single indicator. The best assessment combines: Number of moths caught in traps. Trend in trap catches from one week to the next. Percentage of plants carrying eggs. Percentage of plants with recent mines. Number of live larvae. Percentage of infested growing points. Percentage of fruits with holes. Distribution of infestation within the field. Severity of infestation at field edges compared with the interior. Presence of neighboring old or neglected fields. Presence of natural enemies or parasitized eggs. Crop age and time remaining until harvest. Risk increases when rising trap catches coincide with the presence of new eggs, recent mines, live larvae, and infested fruits.

Misconception One: Tuta absoluta Attacks Leaves Only Incorrect. It can infest leaves, petioles, stems, growing points, and fruits. Misconception Two: The Moth Is the Stage That Bores into Fruits Incorrect. The larva bores into fruits, while the moth lays eggs and spreads the infestation. Misconception Three: Failure to See Moths Means the Field Is Pest-Free Incorrect. Moths are small and their activity may increase at specific times, while larvae remain concealed within the plant. Misconception Four: High Trap Catch Equals the Same Percentage of Damage Inaccurate. The trap measures male moth activity, whereas damage depends on eggs, larvae, their location, and crop age. Misconception Five: Every Mine Requires Immediate Spraying Inaccurate. It is necessary to determine whether the mine is recent and contains a live larva, and to assess the distribution of infestation and the other field indicators. Misconception Six: Changing the Trade Name Prevents Resistance Incorrect. Several trade names may contain the same active ingredient or belong to the same IRAC group and mode of action. Misconception Seven: Increasing the Number of Sprays Means Better Control Incorrect. Random spraying may increase resistance, residues, and cost, and may kill natural enemies without achieving sustainable control.

Tuta absoluta is an invasive moth whose larval stage is responsible for direct damage. Larvae begin feeding within leaves and can also attack growing points, stems, and fruits, resulting in overlapping physiological, marketability, and economic damage. The egg and pupa do not cause feeding damage, while the adult moth represents the reproductive and dispersal stage. Distinguishing among these stages is essential for selecting the appropriate control method and timing. The overall severity of the pest results from the combination of several factors: Concealment of larvae within plant tissues. Infestation of multiple plant parts. Overlapping generations. Continued infestation from the nursery through harvest. Movement between fields. Susceptibility of infested fruits to decay and rejection. High capacity to develop insecticide resistance. Difficulty of relying on a single control method. Accordingly, detecting the pest only after mines have spread and fruits have become infested is considered relatively late detection. True success begins by preventing its entry with transplants, monitoring moths and eggs, detecting young larvae before they become established within plant tissues, and then applying an integrated program that combines cultural, biological, behavioral, and chemical measures. References

The pest Tuta absoluta is known by several scientific, international, Arabic, and local names. This multiplicity results from the transfer of the insect among several genera throughout its taxonomic history, its spread across different geographical and linguistic regions, and the diversity of plant parts attacked by its larvae. Some names have focused on the mines formed by the larvae inside tomato leaves, whereas others have emphasized the insect’s ability to bore into stems, growing points, and fruits. Certain names have also been associated with the insect’s region of origin in South America. In Egypt, the pest is primarily known as “Tuta,” while some growers refer to it as “the weevil” or “tomato weevil.” It is also sometimes confused with the pest commonly known in the field as “leaf miner,” although the latter name usually refers to leaf-mining flies belonging to the genus Liriomyza, not to Tuta absoluta. Understanding the pest’s names is therefore directly related to correct diagnosis, because Tuta absoluta and leaf-mining flies differ in taxonomy, morphology, life cycle, damage sites, monitoring methods, and control programs.

The modern taxonomic scientific name of the insect is: Phthorimaea absoluta Meyrick, 1917 The name by which the pest has become widely known worldwide during recent decades is: Tuta absoluta (Meyrick, 1917) Both names refer to the same insect species, not to two different pests. The name Tuta absoluta has been the most widely used in scientific research, extension publications, integrated pest management programs, pheromone traps, and biological control products. A recent taxonomic study subsequently reassigned the insect to the genus Phthorimaea, making its modern taxonomic name Phthorimaea absoluta. [1, 2] For this reason, the insect appears in references and databases under two forms: Phthorimaea absoluta. Tuta absoluta. The name “Tuta absoluta” remains the best-known name among agricultural engineers, growers, and professionals involved in tomato production.

The scientific name consists of two parts: Genus Name Phthorimaea It begins with a capital letter when written in Latin script. Species Name absoluta It begins with a lowercase letter. The genus and species names are written in italics: Phthorimaea absoluta The name of the scientist who described the species and the year of description are not italicized: Phthorimaea absoluta Meyrick, 1917 The author’s name appears in parentheses when the former name is written: Tuta absoluta (Meyrick, 1917) The parentheses indicate that the insect was not originally described within the genus Tuta, but was transferred to it after its original description. After the full name has been written for the first time, it may be abbreviated as: P. absoluta Or, in references using the widely recognized name: T. absoluta

The British entomologist Edward Meyrick described the insect in 1917 based on specimens collected from Peru in South America. At the time of its original description, it was named: Phthorimaea absoluta Meyrick, 1917 It was subsequently reclassified several times as understanding of the relationships among genera in the tribe Gnorimoschemini within the family Gelechiidae changed. Historically, the insect appeared under the following names: 1. Phthorimaea absoluta. 2. Gnorimoschema absoluta. 3. Scrobipalpula absoluta. 4. Scrobipalpuloides absoluta. 5. Tuta absoluta. 6. Phthorimaea absoluta once again. The name Tuta absoluta became widely used after the insect was reassigned to the genus Tuta in 1994. A morphological taxonomic analysis published in 2021 subsequently showed that the species belongs to a group that includes Phthorimaea operculella, the type species of the genus Phthorimaea. Accordingly, the insect was returned to its original genus. [1, 2]

All the following names refer to the same pest: Scientific Name Taxonomic Status Phthorimaea absoluta Meyrick, 1917 Modern taxonomic name Tuta absoluta (Meyrick, 1917) Most widely recognized and commonly used name Gnorimoschema absoluta Former scientific name Scrobipalpula absoluta Former scientific name Scrobipalpuloides absoluta Former scientific name These names do not indicate the existence of different species; rather, they represent the successive stages through which the classification of the species has passed. The former names are important when reviewing older studies, because some research on pest biology, distribution, and control was published before the name Tuta absoluta became widely adopted.

In the database of the European and Mediterranean Plant Protection Organization, the insect is assigned the code: EPPO Code: GNORAB The first part of this code is derived from the former name: Gnorimoschema absoluta The code has remained unchanged despite the transfer of the insect among different genera. This code makes it possible to link records of the same species within international databases regardless of changes in the scientific name. The pest appears in plant-protection databases under: Phthorimaea absoluta. Tuta absoluta. GNORAB. [1]

Tomato leafminer The literal Arabic translation of this name is: Tomato leaf miner The name Tomato leafminer is used in many international references and publications concerning Tuta absoluta. The name describes the ability of the larvae to enter between the two leaf layers and feed on internal tissues, resulting in the formation of mined blotches or tunnels. However, the term Leafminer is not a taxonomic name specific to one species. It is a general description applied to any insect whose larvae feed within leaf tissues. Different insect species may therefore carry the name Leafminer while belonging to widely separated orders and families. South American tomato leafminer This means: South American tomato leaf miner Or: Tomato leaf miner of South America The name links: The pest’s region of origin in South America. The larva’s mining behavior within tomato leaves. The descriptor South American reduces confusion with other leaf-mining species. Tomato leafminer moth This means: Leaf-mining tomato moth The name combines the form of the adult insect with the nature of larval damage. The adult insect is a small moth, whereas the larva is the stage that enters plant tissues and forms mines. South American tomato moth This means: South American tomato moth It indicates that the insect: Is a small moth belonging to the order Lepidoptera. Originated in South America. Is economically associated with tomato crops. This name is more accurate than the word “butterfly” when describing the adult insect, because the insect belongs to the group of small moths. Tomato borer This means: Tomato borer This name reflects the fact that the damage caused by Tuta absoluta is not restricted to leaves. The larvae can attack: Leaves. Leaf petioles. Growing points. Stems. Calyces. Fruits. The name Tomato borer describes the boring behavior within the plant more broadly than the name Tomato leafminer. Tomato pinworm The name Tomato pinworm appears in some references associated with Tuta absoluta, but this name is also used primarily for another pest: Keiferia lycopersicella Both pests belong to the family Gelechiidae, and the larvae of both can damage tomato leaves and fruits, but they are two different species. [4] Therefore, the name Tomato pinworm alone does not establish the identity of the pest unless the scientific name is also stated. South American tomato pinworm This means: South American tomato pinworm The phrase South American is added to distinguish Tuta absoluta from other pests known as Tomato pinworm. Nevertheless, the names: Tuta absoluta. South American tomato moth. Remain clearer when referring to this pest.

The pest has spread across large areas of the world and has acquired different local names. Language Common Name Approximate Meaning English Tomato leafminer Tomato leaf miner English South American tomato moth South American tomato moth English Tomato borer Tomato borer French Mineuse de la tomate Tomato leaf miner Spanish Polilla del tomate Tomato moth Spanish Minador del tomate Tomato leaf miner Spanish Gusano minador del tomate Tomato-mining larva Spanish Cogollero del tomate Tomato growing-point borer Italian Tignola del pomodoro Tomato moth Portuguese Traça-do-tomateiro Tomato-plant moth German Tomatenminiermotte Tomato-mining moth Dutch Tomatenmineermot Tomato-mining moth Turkish Domates güvesi Tomato moth These names revolve around three principal elements: 1. Tomato as the principal economic host. 2. Mining or boring within tissues as the larval feeding behavior. 3. The moth as the form of the adult insect.

The pest appears in Arabic references under several names, including: Tuta absoluta. Tomato leaf and fruit borer. Tomato leaf and fruit miner. Tomato miner. Tomato borer. South American tomato moth. South American tomato butterfly. Tomato leaf miner. The name: > Tuta absoluta — Tomato leaf and fruit borer Is among the names that most clearly express both the identity of the pest and the nature of its damage. The term “Tuta absoluta” identifies the insect species, while the description “Tomato leaf and fruit borer” clarifies that the damage is not restricted to leaves alone. Tuta absoluta The name “Tuta absoluta” is the phonetic rendering of the widely recognized scientific name: Tuta absoluta This name has the advantage of directly identifying the pest without confusing it with other mining insects. It is widely used in: Scientific research. Agricultural extension publications. Field-visit reports. Control programs. Trap records. Pheromone traps. Biological control programs. Pesticide and seed companies. Tomato Leaf and Fruit Borer This name describes the principal sites of damage caused by the larvae. They often begin feeding inside leaves and may subsequently attack: Growing points. Leaf petioles. Stems. Flowers. Calyces. Green and ripe fruits. This name reflects the economic severity of the pest because fruit infestation causes direct loss of marketable or processable yield. Tomato Leaf Miner This name is the literal translation of: Tomato leafminer It describes the mines or mined blotches formed by the larvae within leaves. However, this name may cause confusion in Egyptian field usage because the terms “leaf miner” or “leaf-miner” are commonly used to refer to leaf-mining flies of the genus Liriomyza. Furthermore, describing Tuta absoluta only as a leaf miner does not include its damage to stems, growing points, and fruits. Tomato Miner This name describes the larva’s behavior within the tomato plant in general, but by itself it does not identify the pest species because tomatoes are attacked by several insects that may bore or mine inside leaves, stems, or fruits. The specific name should therefore always be linked to Tuta absoluta: The tomato miner, Tuta absoluta. South American Tomato Moth This name describes both the adult insect and its region of origin. The adult is a small moth belonging to the family Gelechiidae, while South America is its native region before its spread into Europe, Africa, Asia, and the Middle East.

sectionTitle: Names of the Pest in Egypt
text: Tuta “Tuta” is the most widely used name among growers and agricultural engineers in Egypt. It is an abbreviation of the name: Tuta absoluta Common field expressions include: Tuta infestation. Tuta moth. Tuta larva. Tuta eggs. Tuta trap. Tuta pheromone. Tuta count. Tuta control program. The name “Tuta” refers to the pest in all of its stages, although each stage has a different function: The egg represents the beginning of the generation. The larva causes damage. The pupa is the transformation stage. The adult moth is responsible for mating, dispersal, and egg laying. Tuta Moth The expression “Tuta butterfly” is used to refer to the winged adult insect. The more accurate entomological term is: Tuta moth Because the adult insect is small and belongs to the moth group. The adult moth does not mine into leaves or fruits; it mates and lays eggs. Direct damage begins after the eggs hatch and the larvae emerge. Tuta Worm Growers use the expression “Tuta worm” for the stage found inside leaves or fruits. The scientific name for this stage is: Tuta larva It is the stage responsible for: Forming mines in leaves. Boring into growing points and stems. Penetrating fruits. Producing holes and frass. Opening entry points for secondary rots. Tomato Miner The name “tomato miner” is used in Egyptian extension and technical reports and describes the feeding behavior of larvae within plant tissues. These larvae differ from larvae that feed openly on the leaf surface because they spend a large part of their feeding period protected within: The leaf. The stem. The growing point. The fruit. The Weevil or Tomato Weevil Some growers refer to Tuta absoluta as: The weevil. Tomato weevil. Fruit weevil. Particularly when they observe holes or larvae inside the fruits. This is a popular name that does not reflect the true taxonomic classification of the pest. Tuta absoluta belongs to: Order Lepidoptera The order that includes moths and butterflies. True weevils belong to: Order Coleoptera The order that includes beetles and weevils. Characteristic Tuta absoluta True Weevils Order Lepidoptera Coleoptera Adult insect Small moth Beetle Damaging stage Lepidopteran larva Varies according to species Forewings Membranous and covered with scales Usually hardened Field name Tuta Weevil Accordingly, Tuta absoluta is not a true weevil, even if this name is used for it in some agricultural areas.

sectionTitle: Difference Between Tuta and Leaf Miner in Tomato
text: Meaning of the Term Leaf Miner The term: Leaf miner Literally means: An insect that produces mines in leaves or a leaf-mining insect. It is not the scientific name of a single insect species, but a description of the way in which a larva feeds inside leaf tissue. The mining insect may be: A fly. A moth. A beetle. An insect belonging to another order. Therefore, the presence of a mine within a leaf is not sufficient by itself to identify the insect species. What Is Meant by Leaf Miner in Egyptian Fields? When the name “leaf miner” is used in Egyptian tomato fields, it usually refers to: Leaf-mining flies — Liriomyza spp. Known species within this genus include: Liriomyza trifolii. Liriomyza sativae. Liriomyza bryoniae. These insects belong to: Order Diptera. Family Agromyzidae. The adult is a small fly, usually black and yellow, while the larva lacks visible legs and resembles a small white or yellow maggot. The larvae of Liriomyza form thin, winding, or serpentine mines within leaves. [3] Tuta absoluta Is Not a Leaf-Mining Fly Tuta absoluta belongs to: Order Lepidoptera. Family Gelechiidae. The adult insect is a small moth, not a fly. The larva is a lepidopteran larva with: A distinct head. Thoracic legs. Abdominal prolegs. A body whose color changes with age. The larvae usually form broad, blotch-like, and irregular mines within leaves, and dark frass may be present inside the mine. They can also move from leaves to stems, growing points, and fruits. [5] Comparison Between Tuta and Liriomyza Leaf Miner Comparison Tuta absoluta Liriomyza Leaf Miner Scientific name Phthorimaea absoluta Liriomyza spp. Field name Tuta Leaf miner or leaf-mining flies Order Lepidoptera Diptera Adult insect Small moth Small fly Larval form Lepidopteran larva with a head and legs Legless fly larva Mine shape Usually blotch-like, broad, and irregular Usually narrow, winding, or serpentine Frass Accumulates within parts of the mine Appears as a line or dots within the mine track Infested parts Leaves, stems, growing points, and fruits Primarily leaves Fruit infestation Common and economically important Not a typical form of damage Pheromone trap Used for Tuta Tuta pheromone is not used Adult form Moth Fly Appearance of Tuta Mines Tuta infestation may begin as a narrow mine and then expand as the larva grows, becoming: A translucent blotch. A broad mine. An irregular white or gray area. A blotch containing dark frass. Dry brown tissue in old infestation. Several mines may merge and damage a large portion of the leaflet. The larva can leave one mine and move to another leaf, stem, or fruit. Empty mines may therefore be found alongside mines containing live larvae. Appearance of Liriomyza Mines The mines formed by Liriomyza flies begin as thin lines and gradually widen as the larva grows. They are commonly characterized as: Winding. Serpentine. Relatively long. Confined to the leaf. Containing a trail of frass within the mine. Some mines may widen or overlap under severe infestation, but they usually retain the characteristic winding appearance. [3]

sectionTitle: Difference Between Tuta and Other Similar Pests
text: Tuta and Tomato Fruitworm Tomato fruitworm is commonly associated with the species: Helicoverpa armigera Its larva is larger than the larva of Tuta absoluta, feeds more openly, and produces relatively large holes in fruits. The Tuta larva is smaller, usually begins within leaves, and then enters the fruit through a small opening that may be located near the calyx or elsewhere on the fruit surface. Tuta and Potato Tuber Moth The potato tuber moth is: Phthorimaea operculella Whereas Tuta absoluta is: Phthorimaea absoluta They are two different species within the same genus. The potato tuber moth is primarily associated with potatoes and potato tubers, whereas tomato is the principal economic host of Tuta absoluta. There is some resemblance between the adults of the two species, so accurate identification requires examination of diagnostic characters or the use of species-specific pheromone traps. Tuta and Tomato Pinworm The tomato pinworm of North America is known as: Keiferia lycopersicella Its larvae may form blotch mines in leaves and subsequently attack fruits. This insect differs from Tuta absoluta, despite the fact that both belong to the family Gelechiidae and cause similar types of damage. [4]

Term Meaning Tuta Tuta absoluta in all of its life stages Tuta moth Winged adult insect Tuta larva The mining stage responsible for damage Tuta eggs Eggs laid by females on the plant Tuta pupa Transformation stage before adult emergence Tuta mine A mined blotch or area within the leaf Tuta fruit infestation Larval holes or tunnels inside fruits Leaf miner Leaf-mining flies, Liriomyza spp. Weevil An inaccurate popular name for Tuta Tuta pheromone Sex pheromone specifically attracting male Tuta moths Tuta trap A trap containing a pheromone specific to the pest

sectionTitle: Importance of Correct Naming in Diagnosis and Control
text: Accurate naming is not merely a linguistic issue; it is directly linked to the integrated pest management program. Confusing Tuta with leaf-mining flies may lead to: Using a pheromone unrelated to the pest present. Incorrect interpretation of trap results. Selecting an unsuitable insecticide or application timing. Confusing a lepidopteran larva with a fly larva. Neglecting inspection of fruits and stems. Recording inaccurate monitoring data. Applying a control program targeting a different insect. Incorrectly evaluating the effectiveness of natural enemies. When mines are found in tomato leaves, diagnosis should include examination of: Mine shape. Presence of the larva inside the mine. Shape of the larval head and legs. Presence of infestation in growing points or stems. Presence of holes in fruits. Appearance of the adult insect. Results of pheromone traps. Mine shape alone does not identify the pest in every case, particularly during the early stages of infestation or when several mines overlap.

The modern taxonomic scientific name of Tuta absoluta is: Phthorimaea absoluta Meyrick, 1917 The name most widely recognized worldwide and in the field is: Tuta absoluta (Meyrick, 1917) Its international common names include: Tomato leafminer. South American tomato leafminer. South American tomato moth. Tomato leafminer moth. Tomato borer. South American tomato pinworm. Its Arabic names include: Tuta absoluta. Tomato leaf and fruit borer. Tomato miner. South American tomato moth. Tomato leaf miner. In Egypt, it is mainly known as: Tuta. Tuta moth. Tuta worm or larva. Tomato miner. Weevil, which is a popular but taxonomically incorrect name. Tuta absoluta does not represent “leaf miner” in the common Egyptian field sense. The term leaf miner usually refers to leaf-mining flies of the genus Liriomyza, which are small flies belonging to the order Diptera. Tuta absoluta, in contrast, is a moth belonging to the order Lepidoptera. Its larvae form blotch mines in leaves and also bore into growing points, stems, and fruits. The principal field distinction is therefore: > Tuta: a moth whose larvae bore into leaves, growing points, stems, and fruits. > Leaf miner: a fly whose larvae primarily form winding mines inside leaves. References

Scientific classification is the system that biologists use to arrange organisms into hierarchical groups, starting with broad groups that include large numbers of organisms, then gradually narrowing down to genus and species. Classification is not limited to giving a name to the insect, but rather clarifies its location among living organisms and its degree of relationship to other species. It also helps to understand their anatomical characteristics, life cycle, feeding behavior, and methods of diagnosing and combating them. Tuta absoluta belongs to the arthropods, then to the insects, then to the order Lepidoptera, which includes moths and butterflies. It is placed within the family Gelechiidae, a group that includes small moths, many of whose larvae feed inside plant tissue. The species is currently classified under the name: Phthorimaea absoluta Meyrick, 1917 While the previous name remains: Tuta absoluta (Meyrick, 1917) It is the most famous name in the agricultural field and applied research.

Taxonomic rank Scientific name Arabic name Kingdom Kingdom Animalia Animal kingdom Division Phylum Arthropoda Arthropods Under Division Subphylum Hexapoda Hexapods Sect Class Insecta Insects Rank Order Lepidoptera Lepidoptera Superfamily Superfamily Gelechioidea Geliquidia Platoon Family Gelechiidae The gelikidi Subfamily Subfamily Gelechiinae Jellykini Tribe Tribe Gnorimoschemini gnoremoshimini Sex Genus Phthorimaea Phthurimaya Type Species Phthorimaea absoluta Tuta absoluta, or tomato leaf and fruit miner The classification scale can be represented briefly as follows: Animalia↓Arthropoda↓Hexapoda↓Insecta↓Lepidoptera↓Gelechioidea↓Gelechiidae↓Gelechiinae↓Gnorimoschemini↓Phthorimaea↓Phthorimaea absoluta

sectionTitle: 4.3 A simplified explanation of the classification levels
text: 4.3.1 Animal Kingdom Kingdom: Animalia Tuta absoluta belongs to the animal kingdom because it is a multicellular organism and is not autotrophic, that is, it cannot manufacture its own food as plants do. The insect gets its food from other organisms. In the larval stage, it feeds on plant tissues, especially tomato tissues, while its body does not carry out photosynthesis or produce organic materials from water and carbon dioxide. Tuta absoluta shares general characteristics with other animals, including: Reliance on organic food. The presence of specialized cells. The ability to move at one or more stages of the life cycle. Responding to environmental influences. The presence of sensory and nervous systems. Sexual reproduction. The animal kingdom includes very diverse groups, such as mammals, birds, fish, molluscs, worms, and insects. 4.3.2 Phylum Arthropoda Phylum: Arthropoda The insect belongs to the phylum Arthropoda, one of the largest animal groups in terms of number of species. Arthropods are characterized by several basic characteristics: The presence of an external structure. A body made up of rings or parts. Articulated limbs. Body growth through moulting. Having lateral symmetry. Development of specialized sensory and nervous systems. Exoskeleton The insect's body is covered by an exoskeleton that mainly contains chitin. This cover provides: Mechanical protection. Support for the body. Muscle fulcrums. Reducing water loss. Partial protection from environmental factors. But the exoskeleton does not expand constantly, so the larva needs to molt several times in order to increase its body size. Articulated limbs Arthropods are named because they have legs and appendages made up of parts connected by joints, allowing precise movement. The phylum Arthropoda includes: Insects. Spiders. mites. Crustaceans. Polypods. Although all of these groups are arthropods, Tuta absoluta is an insect, not an arachnid, arachnid, or crustacean. 4.3.3 Under the phylum Hexapoda Subphylum: Hexapoda Hexapoda refers to animals that have three pairs of true legs, meaning six legs. This characteristic appears clearly in the adult insect, where the six legs are located on the chest area. The entire body of an insect consists of three main regions: Head. Chest. Belly. The chest holds: Three pairs of legs. Two pairs of wings in an adult insect. As for the larvae, it has three pairs of true thoracic legs, in addition to fleshy abdominal legs that help it move. The ventral legs are not true articulated legs like the thoracic legs. 4.3.4 Insect class Class: Insecta Tuta absoluta belongs to the insect class. Adult insects are generally characterized by the presence of: Clear head. A chest made of three rings. An abdomen made up of several rings. Three pairs of legs. A pair of antennae. Compound eyes. Specialized mouth parts. One or two pairs of wings in most adult species. Insects differ from arachnids and spiders in the number of legs and body division. Adult insects have six legs, while adult mites and spiders usually have eight legs. Tuta absoluta undergoes complete transformation during its life cycle, and goes through four distinct phases: The egg. Caterpillar. Virgo. The whole insect. The form, behavior and function of the larva differ significantly from that of the adult insect. The larvae is the feeding and damage phase, while the adult insect represents the mating, propagation, and egg-laying phase.

Order: Lepidoptera Tuta absoluta belongs to the order Lepidoptera, the order that includes moths and butterflies. The name Lepidoptera consists of two Greek roots that stand for: Scales. wings. This is due to the insect's entire wings being covered with fine microscopic scales. General characteristics of Lepidoptera Insects belonging to this order are characterized by several characteristics, including: The presence of two pairs of wings in adult insects. Cover the wings and body with fine scales. Having a complete transformation. The presence of a worm-like larval stage. The presence of a clear head in the larva. The larvae have three pairs of thoracic legs. Most larvae have ventral legs. Transforming into a pupa before the adult insect emerges. Tuta absoluta belongs to the group of small moths, and is not a fly. This point clearly distinguishes them from tunneling flies of the genus Liriomyza, which belong to the order Diptera Diptera. Why is it a moth and not a diurnal butterfly? There are general differences between diurnal moths and butterflies, although both belong to the order Lepidoptera. The Tuta absoluta moth is characterized by: Small in size. Not bright gray or brown colours. They are more active in darkness or low light. Their wings are relatively narrow. It has wings with long bristles. It hides during the day among the leaves of plants. Therefore, the expression “Tuta moth” is more accurately entomological than the expression “Tuta moth,” even though the two expressions are understandable in field use.

The family Tuta absoluta is located within the superfamily Gelechioidea. This group includes a large number of small moths, many of which are referred to as the group of micromoths or: Microlepidoptera Calling them microscopic moths does not mean that they are microscopic organisms, but rather that adult insects are small compared to larger butterflies and moths. Many species within this group are characterized by the following: Small size of adult insect. Narrow wings. The presence of hairs on the edges of the wings. Accurate diagnosis depends on microscopic characteristics. Larvae feed inside plant tissues or protected parts. Relative specialization in some plant families. These characteristics explain why Tuta absoluta is difficult to identify based on size and color alone because several small moths may look similar in appearance.

Tuta absoluta belongs to the family Gelechiidae. It is a large family of small moths, and its members are sometimes called: Gelechiid moths The family includes multiple species associated with plants and agricultural crops. General characteristics of the species Adult insects in this family are often characterized by the following: Small size. Thin body. Relatively narrow front wings. Hind wings with long hairy edges. Relatively long antennae. Gray or brown colors help with camouflage. Nocturnal activity or activity in low-light periods. As for the larvae, in many species they feed in protected places, such as: inside the leaves. Inside the stems. Inside the buds. inside the fruits. Inside the tubers. Between leaves wrapped or tied with silk threads. This pattern is clearly visible in Tuta absoluta; The larvae burrow into tomato leaves, and also attack the tops, stems, and fruits. The agricultural importance of the species The Gelechiidae species includes a number of important agricultural pests, such as: Tuta absoluta, or tomato leaf and fruit miner. Potato tuber moth Phthorimaea operculella. Tomato pinworm Keiferia lycopersicella. Other species infect grains, leaves, seeds and fruits. The presence of Tuta absoluta and the potato tuber moth within the same family explains some of the similarities between them, but it does not mean that they are the same species.

Tuta absoluta falls within the subfamily Gelechiinae, one of the inbred groups of the subfamily Gelechiidae. The subfamily level is used to divide large families into smaller groups, comprising genera and species that share a more specific set of anatomical and evolutionary characters. Differences between subspecies are not easily apparent in field examination, but often depend on: Installing and veining the wings. Shape of mouth parts. Characteristics of the head and antennae. Characteristics of the reproductive organs. Larval traits. Evolutionary and molecular analyses. Therefore, identifying the infestation in the field may reach the genus or species level through symptoms, larvae, and traps, while accurate taxonomic confirmation requires a specialist or laboratory examination.

The insect belongs within the subfamily Gelechiinae to the tribe: Gnorimoschemini A tribe is a taxonomic order located between subfamily and genus. This tribe includes a group of small moths whose larvae are associated with a number of plants, especially some species of the Solanaceae family and other herbaceous plants. Within this tribe there are closely related races, including: Phthorimaea. Tuta. Gnorimoschema. Scrobipalpula. Scrobipalpuloides. Keiferia. Symmetrischema.
