The convergence between these genera led to the classification of Tuta absoluta changing several times, as it was historically transferred between: Phthorimaea. Gnorimoschema. Scrobipalpula. Scrobipalpuloides. Tuta. The taxonomic revision published in 2021 returned the species to the genus Phthorimaea, after morphological analysis showed its presence in an evolutionary group that includes Phthorimaea operculella.

A genus is a group of closely related species, sharing more specific anatomical and evolutionary traits than they do at the species or tribe level. The insect currently belongs to the genus: Phthorimaea It is a genus that includes small moths, a number of which are related to plants of the Solanaceae family. The most famous agricultural species within this genus are: Phthorimaea absoluta Tomato leaf and fruit miner, known as Tuta absoluta. Phthorimaea operculella Potato tuber moth, which is an important pest that infects potato leaves and tubers in the field and warehouse. The relationship between the two types Adjective Phthorimaea absoluta Phthorimaea operculella Common name Tuta absoluta Potato tuber moth The main economic breadwinner Tomatoes Potatoes The most important damage Leaves, tops, stems and fruits Leaves, stems and tubers Pheromone trap Tuta-specific pheromone A pheromone specific to the tuber moth Type absoluta operculella Both pests may be found in the same agricultural area, especially when there are tomatoes and potatoes, so it is not enough to rely on seeing a small moth to determine the type.

Species is the most precise major level of scientific classification used to identify an organism. The full name of the species is: Phthorimaea absoluta Meyrick, 1917 The first part refers to gender: Phthorimaea While the second part refers to the qualitative characteristic: absoluta absoluta is not written alone as a full scientific name because the correct name consists of the genus name and the specific character together. This type is characterized by a comprehensive set of characteristics, including: The external appearance of the egg, larva, pupa and moth. Installing and veining the wings. Shape of body scales and wings. Characteristics of male and female reproductive organs. Molecular characteristics and genetic material. Sex pheromone synthesis. Biobehaviour. Strong association with tomato and Solanaceae families. Nature of tunnels and damage. Scientific identification of the species does not depend on only one symptom, such as the presence of a tunnel in a leaf. Because several insects can form similar tunnels.

sectionTitle: 4.11 Why did the gender change from Tuta to Phthorimaea?
text: The type was known for many years as: Tuta absoluta However, its position within tribe Gnorimoschemini has remained a subject of taxonomic debate due to the similarity between several genera within this group. Modern taxonomic revision has relied on the analysis of a set of comparable morphological characters between closely related genera. The analysis showed that the species known as Tuta absoluta is in a monophyletic group that includes: Phthorimaea operculella It is the typical species of the genus Phthorimaea. Based on this relationship, the species was returned to its original genus, and the name became: Phthorimaea absoluta Meyrick, 1917 As for the genus Tuta, it remains a taxonomically valid genus, but it does not include this species according to the recent review. The difference in the two names does not indicate a change in the insect or the nature of its damage, but rather a modification of its position within the classification system.

sectionTitle: 4.12 A simplified explanation of the idea of classification
text: Scientific classification can be likened to a graduated title that precisely identifies the location of an insect. If a species represents an identifiable individual, the higher levels are something like: State. Governorate. Center. The village. Street. Home. The lower we go the definition becomes more specific. For Tuta absoluta: Animalia: tells us it's an animal. Arthropoda: tells us it is an arthropod with an exoskeleton. Hexapoda: tells us it is a hexapod. Insecta: tells us it's an insect. Lepidoptera: tells us it is a moth or butterfly with scaly wings. Gelechiidae: places it among the small moths of this family. Gnorimoschemini: links it to a finer group of closely related moths. Phthorimaea: specifies its gender. Phthorimaea absoluta: specifies the same type individually. Thus, classification does not mean merely memorizing Latin names, but rather gradually clarifies the identity of the insect and its relationship to other organisms.

sectionTitle: 4.13 Taxonomic difference between Tuta and Leafminer
text: The classification helps clarify that Tuta absoluta is radically different from the tunneling flies known in the field as leaf miners. Rank Tuta absoluta Leafminer flies Kingdom Animalia Animalia Division Arthropoda Arthropoda Sect Insecta Insecta Rank Lepidoptera Diptera Platoon Gelechiidae Agromyzidae Sex Phthorimaea Liriomyza Mostly The whole insect Moth Fly Harmful phase Butterfly larva Fly larva The two species are similar in their ability to form tunnels inside leaves, but similarity in damage does not mean direct taxonomic affinity. Tuta absoluta is taxonomically closer to the potato tuber moth than to the Liriomyza fly, although both may infest tomato leaves.

sectionTitle: 4.14 The taxonomic difference between the Tuta and the true weevil
text: Some farmers give the name “weevil” to Tuta, but the classification shows that this name is scientifically incorrect. Rank Tuta absoluta Real weevils Sect Insecta Insecta Rank Lepidoptera Coleoptera The whole insect Moth Beetle wings Covered with scales Often stiff front Caterpillar Butterfly larva Beetle larva Transformation Complete Complete Tuta is a lepidopteran insect, whereas true weevils belong to the order Coleoptera.

sectionTitle: 4.15 The importance of classification in integrated management
text: Pheromone identification Each species has a special pheromone composition, and the Tuta absoluta pheromone is not suitable for detecting all moths or leafminers. Diagnosis of larvae The Lepidopteran butterfly larvae differ from the Diptera fly larvae in: Head shape. Having legs. Caterpillar movement. Shape of mouth parts. Feeding behaviour. Choose vital enemies Some parasites and predators are associated with specific groups or stages, so species identification influences the selection of the biological control organism. Pesticide resistance management Sites of action and effective groups vary depending on the type of pest. Resistance registers and IRAC group rotation programs are also based on selecting the correct type. Prevent confusion between traps Tuta absoluta, potato tuber moth and other types of small moths may be found in the agricultural area. Taxonomic identification prevents all moths in a trap from being interpreted as a single species. Agricultural quarantine The competent authorities use the scientific name and international symbol to identify the pest in: Phytosanitary certificates. Survey reports. Propagation records. Quarantine procedures. International notifications. Examination of seedlings and fruits. Tuta Absoluta has the code EPPO: GNORAB It is a fixed code that allows the pest to be tracked even when its scientific name changes.

Daily field inspection does not require memorization of all taxonomic ranks, but depends on understanding the group to which the pest belongs. When diagnosing Tuta absoluta in the field, a set of indicators are examined: Form of paper tunnels. The presence of waste inside the tunnels. The shape of the caterpillar and its head. The presence of thoracic and abdominal legs. Infestation of tops and stems. The presence of holes in the fruits. Complete moth form. Results of the pheromone trap. Nature of the plant host. Laboratory diagnosis when in doubt. The specialized taxonomic diagnosis may depend on: Characteristics of the reproductive organs. Microscopic characteristics of adult insects. Fine larval characteristics. Molecular fingerprinting. Sequencing parts of DNA. Mitochondrial genome structure. Laboratory examination becomes necessary when the pest is discovered in a new area or when there are apparently similar species.

Tuta absoluta belongs to the kingdom Animalia, phylum Arthropoda, and class Insecta. It is a moth of the order Lepidoptera, not a fly or a mite. Its complete classification is as follows: Kingdom: Animalia. Division: Arthropoda. Under Division: Hexapoda. Sect: Insecta. Rank: Lepidoptera. Supratype: Gelechioidea. Species: Gelechiidae. Subfamily: Gelechiinae. Tribe: Gnorimoschemini. Gender: Phthorimaea. Type: Phthorimaea absoluta. The full modern scientific name is: Phthorimaea absoluta Meyrick, 1917 The name widely known in the agricultural field is: Tuta absoluta (Meyrick, 1917) The classification proves that: Tuta is a Lepidopteran moth. Leafminer Liriomyza Diptera fly. A true weevil is a beetle of the order Coleoptera. The potato tuber moth is a closely related species of Tuta within the genus Phthorimaea, but it is not the same species. This classification allows precise identification of the pest, preventing confusion with other insects, and linking field diagnosis with appropriate means of monitoring, control, and agricultural quarantine. References 1.European and Mediterranean Plant Protection Organization (EPPO). Phthorimaea absoluta (GNORAB): Taxonomic classification. https://gd.eppo.int/taxon/GNORAB 2. Chang, P. E. C., & Metz, M. A. (2021). Classification of Tuta absoluta (Meyrick, 1917) (Lepidoptera: Gelechiidae: Gelechiinae: Gnorimoschemini) based on cladistic analysis of morphology. Proceedings of the Entomological Society of Washington, 123(1), 41–54. https://doi.org/10.4289/0013-8797.123.1.41 3.GBIF Secretariat. Phthorimaea absoluta Meyrick, 1917: Species record and taxonomic classification. https://www.gbif.org/species/4528100 4. USDA–APHIS IDtools. Tuta absoluta: Microlepidoptera on Solanaceae—Identification Key. https://idtools.org/tools/1035/index.cfm?entityID=2869&packageID=1101 5. USDA–APHIS Cooperative Agricultural Pest Survey Program. Phthorimaea absoluta CAPS Datasheet. https://caps.ceris.purdue.edu/wp-content/uploads/2025/07/Phthorimaea-absoluta-CAPS-datasheet-20250328.pdf

The story of the spread of Tuta absoluta represents one of the most rapid bioinvasions seen in modern agricultural pests. For many years, the insect remained associated with South America, then it appeared in Spain in 2006, and from there it spread within a short period to most regions of the Mediterranean basin, Europe, Africa, the Middle East, and Asia. In less than two decades, the pest has transformed from a well-known regional problem in South American countries into a global threat to tomato production in open fields and protected crops. Its spread was not the result of the insect's ability to fly alone, but rather resulted from the interaction of several factors, the most important of which are: International trade in tomato fruits and plant materials. Movement of seedlings, containers and means of transportation. Natural transition between adjacent areas. Continued cultivation of tomatoes and other solanaceous crops throughout the year. The ability of an insect to produce successive generations. Eggs and larvae hide in or on plant tissue. Suitability of the climate of the Mediterranean, Africa and Asia for its establishment. Delayed detection of infestation in new areas. Poor agricultural hygiene procedures and crop waste disposal. A distinction must be made between the insect’s actual introduction date and its first official record. The pest may reach an area and remain at low numbers for weeks or months before it is detected and diagnosed. The record years cited in this chapter therefore represent the first documented detection and do not always prove the exact date on which the first individual entered the country.

5.2.1 Geographic origin Tuta absoluta is a pest of Neotropical origin, and its original habitat is located in South America, especially the western part of the Neotropical region, or what is known as: Western Neotropics Its presence has been historically linked to the areas extending on both sides of the Andes Mountains and tomato production areas in South American countries. The pest has been recorded in its original habitat in countries including: Peru. Chile. Argentina. Bolivia. Brazil. Colombia. Ecuador. Paraguay. Uruguay. Venezuela. Therefore, the insect is not introduced or invasive within most regions of South America, but rather represents an indigenous part of the pest community associated with tomatoes and the Solanaceae family in the region. 5.2.2 First scientific description in Peru British entomologist Edward Merrick described the insect scientifically in 1917, based on specimens collected in the Huancayo region of Peru. When it was first described, it bore the name: Phthorimaea absoluta Meyrick, 1917 Then its classification position changed several times, until it became known internationally as: Tuta absoluta Before it was recently returned to the genus Phthorimaea. The description of the insect in 1917 does not mean that it appeared in Peru during that year, but rather it means that this is the date of its official scientific identification and granting it a taxonomic name. 5.2.3 Its spread within South America During the twentieth century, the insect became one of the most important pests of tomatoes in South America. By the last decades of the twentieth century, it was registered in most of the major tomato-producing countries on the continent. It helped spread it within South America: Growing tomatoes on large areas. Overlapping planting dates. Trading fruits and seedlings between regions. The presence of potatoes, eggplant, and other solanaceous families. Temperature suitability. The ability of butterflies to move between fields. Persistence of volunteer plants and host weeds. The pest has become a major problem in both fresh and industrial tomatoes, especially in Argentina, Brazil, Chile and Peru. 5.2.4 Environmental balance in the original habitat In South America, the insect evolved for long periods within an environment containing a variety of biological enemies, including: Egg parasites. Larval parasites. Predators. Entomopathogenic fungi. Natural pathogens. This is why Tuta absoluta is connected in its original habitat to a more complex ecological network than in the areas it has recently invaded. But the presence of biological enemies did not prevent it from becoming an economic pest, especially when intensive spraying programs led to the killing of predators and parasites and the emergence of resistance to pesticides. The accumulated experience in South America is an important basis for understanding: Pest resistance to pesticides. Efficiency of larval parasitology. The role of predators. Effect of resistant varieties. The importance of integrated management.

sectionTitle: 5.3 Moving from South America to Europe
text: 5.3.1 How did the insect leave its original habitat? The route by which Tuta absoluta crossed from South America to Europe has not been conclusively settled. Pest risk analyzes suggest that entry may have occurred through agricultural trade, particularly: Infected tomato fruits. Fruits bearing eggs or larvae near the calyx. Packages and packing boxes. Sorting waste accompanying shipments. Infected plants or seedlings. Plant materials from the Solanaceae family. Tuta absoluta moths have been found in supermarkets and European packing stations that receive imported fruit, demonstrating the ability of fruit trading chains to transmit the pest. However, proving the presence of the insect in a filling station does not alone prove that this station was the source of the first spread. The exact route of entry into Spain therefore remained uncertain. 5.3.2 First record in Europe: Spain 2006 Tuta absoluta was first recorded in Europe at the end of 2006, in tomato fields in the Castellón district of the Valencia region in eastern Spain. In 2007, the infestation appeared in other locations on the Spanish Mediterranean coast, especially in the province of Valencia. The pest was also discovered on the island of Ibiza in the Balearic Islands. Some winter crops were severely infected, which made it clear early on that the pest can: Establishment in the Mediterranean climate. Reproduction in greenhouses and fields. Moving between production areas. Causing large losses in a short time. The Spanish record represented the fundamental turning point in the global history of the pest, as the Iberian Peninsula became the gateway for its spread to the Mediterranean basin and Afro-Eurasia. 5.3.3 Rapid spread within Spain After the first discovery, the pest did not remain confined to Castellón, but rather moved to tomato-producing areas on the Spanish Mediterranean coast. This helped: Tomato planting density. Interconnection between production areas. Movement of fruits and packages. The presence of agricultural greenhouses. Mild winters in coastal areas. Butterflies moving between fields. Continue planting suitable hosts. Within a short period, the pest became present in most Spanish tomato-producing regions, including the Canary Islands.

sectionTitle: 5.4 Distribution of Tuta absoluta in Europe
text: 5.4.1 Distribution in Western and Southern Europe After being recorded in Spain, the insect quickly moved east and north through tomato production and marketing networks. The most prominent stages of European expansion are: Spain: First record at the end of 2006. France: discoveries during 2008. Italy: First recorded in spring 2008 in Calabria, then spread to multiple Italian regions. Portugal: Officially registered in 2009. Malta: Registered in 2009. Switzerland: Registered in 2009. Greece: discovered in 2009. Cyprus: Discovered in protected tomato production areas in 2009. United Kingdom: The emergence of foci inside tomato production and trading facilities during 2009. Germany, the Netherlands and other Nordic countries: records of individuals or localized foci, often associated with packing stations or protected cultivations. Within a few years, the insect reached more than thirty countries within Europe and the western Antarctic region. 5.4.2 More than one propagation path The spread of Tuta absoluta in Europe did not result entirely from a continuous advance of butterflies from Spain to neighboring countries. In some countries geographically distant foci appeared at roughly the same time, as in Greece, where the nature of the distribution suggested the possibility of several separate introductions rather than a gradual, natural spread from a single foci. This shows the existence of two overlapping paths: Natural spread It occurs through the flight of butterflies between adjacent areas and fields. Human-assisted spread It occurs through the transmission of: The fruits. Seedlings. Packages. Vehicles. Sorting waste. Plant products between countries and regions. This pattern explains the insect's appearance in locations far from the nearest known focus. 5.4.3 Southern Europe and Northern Europe The chances of the pest becoming established were higher in southern European countries with a Mediterranean climate, where: Moderate winter temperatures. Open and protected tomato cultivations. Long production seasons. High density of fields and greenhouses. In some northern European countries, low temperatures have limited their ability to continue in open fields throughout the year. But heated greenhouses, packing plants and supermarkets provide environments in which the pest can survive or appear as temporary localized foci. Therefore, the situation differed between European countries. The scourge was: Well established and widespread in some countries. Limited in certain regions of other countries. Transient or subject to eradication in some cold regions.

5.5.1 North Africa in 2008 Tuta absoluta crossed the Mediterranean shortly after its appearance in Spain. It was recorded during 2008 in three main countries of the Maghreb: Morocco. Algeria. Tunisia. These records represented the beginning of the African invasion of the pest. The geographical and commercial proximity between southern Europe and North Africa helped its movement, especially with the intensity of the tomato trade and the presence of connected production areas on the coasts of the Mediterranean. After arriving in the Maghreb, it moved east towards Libya and Egypt, then south to regions of sub-Saharan Africa.

sectionTitle: 5.6 The entry of Tuta Absoluta into Egypt in 2009
text: 5.6.1 First record in Egypt The first documented observations of Tuta absoluta were recorded in Egypt in 2009. Egyptian studies indicate that the first record occurred in Marsa Matrouh Governorate, located in northwestern Egypt near the Libyan border and the Mediterranean coast. Then the pest appeared during 2010 in other regions, and then spread rapidly among tomato-producing governorates. The database of the European and Mediterranean Organization for Plant Protection records the status of the pest in Egypt as follows: First record: 2009. Spread after that: Rapidly nationwide. Status: Present and widespread. 5.6.2 Did the insect enter Egypt from Libya? The geographical location of the first record in Marsa Matruh is consistent with the possibility of the pest moving from the West, after it spread in the Maghreb and Libya. Transmission may have occurred through: Movement of fruits and containers across the coast and borders. Regional trade. Means of transportation. Butterflies gradually move between agricultural areas. More than one track at the same time. But this geographical sequence does not represent conclusive evidence that Libya was the only direct source of the insect's introduction into Egypt. There is not enough evidence to determine: The shipment that transported the pest. Exact entry point. Date of arrival of the first individual. Whether the entry occurred once or multiple times. Therefore, it remains scientifically certain that the pest was discovered in Egypt in 2009, and then spread rapidly, while the direct entry route remains not definitively decided. 5.6.3 Reasons for its rapid spread within Egypt Tuta absoluta found in the Egyptian agricultural environment very suitable conditions for settlement and reproduction. Multiple tomato shoots Tomatoes in Egypt are grown during multiple shifts and dates, providing plants suitable for the pest during most months of the year. Transporting the crop between governorates Fruits, seedlings, boxes, and means of transportation move between production areas, markets, packing stations, and factories, which increases the chances of transporting live stages. Field ages overlap Sometimes found in the same area: Nurseries. Newly planted fields. Fields in the vegetative growth stage. Fruitful fields. Fields whose harvest has ended and their residues have not been removed. This overlap allows butterflies to constantly move from old to new fields. Climatic conditions Temperatures in large areas of Egypt allow the development of multiple generations, and protected crops and warm areas help activity continue during the winter. Alternative families Potatoes, eggplants and some nightshade weeds provide plants that can help the pest persist between tomato pods. Poor agricultural hygiene Leaving the plants after the end of the harvest results in the continuation of caterpillars and pupae and the emergence of a new generation of butterflies. Early dependence on pesticides The seriousness of the pest has prompted many producers to intensify spraying, but in some cases repeating the same pesticide combinations has led to decreased effectiveness and harm to biological enemies. 5.6.4 The impact of pest introduction on Egyptian tomato production The emergence of Tuta absoluta has significantly changed tomato protection programs in Egypt. Management became dependent on a set of procedures that were not applied with the same regularity before the pest entered, such as: Pheromone traps. Weekly counting of butterflies. Examination of tunnels and larvae. Paying attention to pest-free nurseries. Rotation of pesticide groups. Rapid disposal of crop residues. Monitoring fruit infestation. Application of biological control programs. Consider pesticide residues and the pre-harvest period. Control costs have also increased and crop success has become highly dependent on when the infestation is detected and the efficiency of the integrated programme.

sectionTitle: 5.7 Distribution from North Africa to Sub-Saharan Africa
text: 5.7.1 Direction east and south After the spread of the pest in North African countries, it moved through several paths: From Egypt and Libya towards Sudan and East Africa. From the Maghreb towards West Africa. Through trade and transport networks between capitals and markets. From tomato production centers to neighboring regions. Across coastal and inland countries. It was recorded in Sudan during 2010, and then continued to spread in the Horn of Africa and the east of the continent. 5.7.2 East Africa The pest reached tomato producing areas in East African countries during the first half of the second decade of the twenty-first century. Its presence was recorded in countries such as: Sudan. Ethiopia. Kenya. Tanzania. Uganda. Rwanda. Burundi. In Kenya, butterflies have been collected in pheromone traps since the beginning of 2014, and high numbers have appeared in some tomato-producing areas. The movement of tomatoes between regions and markets, in addition to natural flight, helped in their rapid spread among East African countries. 5.7.3 West Africa Tuta absoluta has also moved to West Africa, where it has appeared in tomato-growing areas in countries including: Senegal. Niger. Nigeria. Boys. Togo. Ghana. Burkina Faso. Other countries in the region. Its spread in West Africa was linked to the movement of fruits across borders, the overlap of regional markets, the weakness of internal quarantine systems, and the presence of successive production seasons. 5.7.4 Central and Southern Africa The pest continued to spread to: Democratic Republic of the Congo. Angola. Zambia. Zimbabwe. Malawi. Mozambique. Botswana. Namibia. South Africa. It was recorded in Mozambique in 2016, then appeared in several provinces from north to south. It was also recorded in South Africa during 2016–2017, completing a journey that extended from the northern coast of the continent to the far south. By the end of the second decade of the twenty-first century, the pest had reached all major African regions during a period of approximately ten years since its first discovery in North Africa.

5.8.1 Türkiye is the gateway to Asian expansion Tuta absoluta was recorded in Türkiye for the first time during August 2009, in the Urla district of Izmir Province on the Aegean coast. The larvae showed: Macular tunnels in leaves. Injuries to the pneumatic parts. Superficial tunnels on the fruits. The insect then spread rapidly throughout Turkish tomato-producing areas, and Türkiye became a major starting point for the pest's eastward spread into Asia. 5.8.2 The Levant The pest appeared during the following period in a number of eastern Mediterranean countries, including: Israel. Palestinian territories. Syria. Lebanon. Jordan. The geographical and commercial interdependence between these countries, in addition to the movement of fruits and seedlings, helped the pest move between tomato production areas. In Syria, butterflies and damage were observed in a number of coastal areas, and then the pest continued to move towards Iraq and the countries of the Arabian Peninsula. 5.8.3 Iraq Tuta absoluta was recorded in Iraq for the first time during the fall of 2010, near the Rabia area in Nineveh Governorate adjacent to the Syrian border. The record location reflects the possibility of its movement via the northwestern route from the Levant and Turkey. Then the pest became recorded in other regions, especially with the movement of tomatoes between governorates and the availability of suitable climatic conditions. 5.8.4 Iran The pest arrived in Iran at the beginning of the second decade of the twenty-first century, and then appeared in a number of tomato-producing regions. Iran represented an important corridor for the pest’s movement towards: Central Asia. Pakistan. East and South Asia regions. The diversity of the Iranian climate and the presence of protected and open crops also helped to stabilize it in several regions. 5.8.5 Arabian Peninsula Tuta absoluta continued to spread throughout the Arabian Peninsula and the Gulf, and was recorded in: Saudi Arabia. Sultanate of Oman. Qatar. United Arab Emirates. Yemen. It was recorded in Qatar in 2012, and in the UAE in 2012 in tomato and eggplant greenhouses. As for Yemen, it was discovered in January 2013, and then official surveys showed its spread in a large number of farms and governorates within a short period. The rapid spread in dry environments indicates that lack of rain does not prevent the insect from settling when: Irrigated crops. Suitable temperatures. Continuous plant hosts. Protected agricultural facilities. Markets transporting fruits between regions.

5.9.1 From Türkiye to Central and South Asia After the insect was recorded in Türkiye in 2009, it moved east along parallel paths: Türkiye, the Levant, Iraq and Iran. Iran and Central Asia. Arabian Peninsula. Pakistan and India. Nepal and Bangladesh. regions of western China. The spread within Asia has been rapid, especially in countries that grow tomatoes year-round or exchange fruits and seedlings across borders. 5.9.2 India Tuta absoluta was recorded in India for the first time in 2014, in areas of the south and west of the country. It then appeared in: regions of central India. Northwest India. Eastern regions. Northeast India. The large area of tomato cultivation, the multiple production seasons, and the movement of fruits between states helped expand the scope of the pest. Its appearance in India became an important event in the course of the invasion because India represents a major center for tomato production and trade in South Asia. 5.9.3 Nepal The insect was officially recorded in Nepal during May 2016 on a commercial tomato farm in the Kathmandu Valley. The diagnosis is confirmed by: External attributes. Examination of male genital organs. Comparison with reference samples. Initial surveys showed that it was not confined to one farm, but had spread throughout the Kathmandu Valley and surrounding areas. 5.9.4 Pakistan, Bangladesh and Central Asia The pest appeared during approximately the same period in other countries of South and Central Asia, including: Pakistan. Bangladesh. Kyrgyzstan. Tajikistan. Uzbekistan. Kazakhstan. The location of these countries on land trade routes, along with the connectivity of tomato-producing areas, facilitated its spread. It was recorded in Kyrgyzstan during the 2010s, and the first documented record appeared in Tajikistan shortly thereafter. 5.9.5 China Tuta absoluta arrived in northwestern China during the second half of the 2010s. The western border regions represented the first entry point for the pest, which is consistent with its movement through Central Asian countries. Its arrival in China raised great concerns because: The large area of tomato production. The presence of large industrial tomato cultivations. Multiple climatic zones. Intensity of internal trade. Possibility of moving from the western regions to the eastern production regions. Climatic suitability models have shown that large areas of China and Asia may allow it to survive or evolve seasonally when the appropriate host and temperature are available.

Year or period Main event 1917 Scientific description of the insect from samples collected in Peru XX century It has spread and established its status as a major pest of tomatoes in South America The beginning of the eighties It is widely recorded in a large number of South American countries End of 2006 First European record in Castellón, Spain 2007 It is spread on the Spanish coast and on the island of Ibiza 2008 It was recorded in Morocco, Algeria, and Tunisia, and appeared in Italy and France 2009 Registered in Egypt, Turkey and a large number of Mediterranean and European countries 2010 It appears in Sudan, Syria, Iraq and several regions of the Middle East 2011–2013 Its expansion into East Africa, the Arabian Peninsula and Iran 2014 Registered in India, Kenya and other African and Asian regions 2015–2016 It is distributed in Nepal, Pakistan, Bangladesh, Mozambique and sub-Saharan Africa 2016–2017 It reaches South Africa, parts of Central Asia and China After 2017 Continued local expansion and the emergence of new records in areas that were free or limited in infestation This table presents selected milestones and is not a complete list for every country or first-record location.

5.11.1 International trade in tomatoes Infected fruits can carry: Eggs near the cup. Larvae inside invisible tunnels. Pupae or larvae hiding in the accompanying detritus. Butterflies inside packages or means of transportation. The fruit may appear outwardly healthy even though there is a small larvae inside, especially at the beginning of the infestation. Trade transports fruits within hours or days to distances far exceeding the natural flight range of the moth. 5.11.2 Movement of seedlings and plants Infected seedlings pose a greater risk than fruit in some cases because it may carry: Eggs on leaves. Larvae inside small tunnels. Maidens between the leaves or in the planting medium. A living plant that allows the pest cycle to be completed. A limited number of infected seedlings can start a new focus within an empty area. 5.11.3 Packaging and transport boxes Live stages may hide in: Boxes. Plastic or wooden containers. Remnants of leaves and cups. Transport vehicle floors. Packing station waste. Sorting and temporary storage units. The risk increases when containers are reused between farms and markets without cleaning. 5.11.4 Natural dispersal of butterflies Adults can fly and move between fields, especially when: High density inside the old field. Stop spraying before removing plants. Drying the crop. Harvest ends. Availability of a fresh field nearby. Wind activity in the direction of agricultural areas. This ability is sufficient to expand the infestation locally between neighboring villages and regions, but it alone does not explain the sudden appearance of the pest in distant countries or islands. 5.11.5 Overlapping tomato growing seasons The pest needs a plant host to continue reproduction. When tomatoes are grown all year round, the butterflies move from one button to another without any apparent interruption. The persistence of host plants between seasons is known as: Green Bridge — Green Bridge This bridge may consist of: Tomato fields of overlapping ages. Nurseries. Volunteer plants. potato. Eggplant. Wild Solanaceae families. Crop residues not removed. 5.11.6 Reproductive ability and multigenerationality Tuta absoluta produces successive generations when heat and hosts are available. Shorter generation times in warm conditions result in: Rapid multiplication of numbers. The pest moves from an unnoticed level to a severe outbreak. Increased possibility of individuals moving with shipments. Speed of colonization of new sites. Overlapping of eggs, caterpillars, pupae and butterflies. The pest does not depend on one generation per year, but can complete multiple generations depending on the climate. 5.11.7 Absence of forced rest Tuta absoluta does not depend on forced winter dormancy that prevents it from being active when suitable conditions exist. When tomatoes and the appropriate temperature are available inside the greenhouse or warm areas, the life cycle can continue during long periods of the year. This feature helps: Staying in protected crops. Winter crossing in temperate regions. Injury onset early in the following season. Continuity of generations in tropical and subtropical regions. 5.11.8 Hiding live stages The pest is difficult to detect at the beginning of the invasion because: The eggs are small. New larvae quickly enter tissues. Early tunnels are limited in size. Larvae may be found inside the fruits. Pupae may be found in soil or detritus. Butterflies are small and hide inside the shoots. The internal feeding nature of the insect sometimes allows it to pass through rapid inspections. 5.11.9 Similarity to other injuries Initial Tuta injury may be confused with: Liriomyza. Leafminer Flies Tomato fruit worm. Potato tuber moth. Leaf spots. Mechanical wounds or rots in fruits. Late diagnosis gives the pest enough time to produce one or more generations before containment measures are initiated. 5.11.10 Climate suitability The conditions of large areas of the world are compatible with the thermal needs of the insect, especially: Mediterranean Basin. North Africa. Irrigated areas in the Middle East. Tropical Africa and temperate highlands. South and Central Asia. Protected areas in cold Europe. Extreme heat does not always mean pest elimination, because growing plants, greenhouses, and irrigation provide microclimates that are less harsh than the open air temperature. 5.11.11 Expanding the range of families Tomatoes are the main economic host, but the pest can survive on other plants, especially from the Solanaceae family. The hosts or related plants include: potato. Eggplant. Some types of pepper in specific conditions. Wild eggplant. Grape Eldib. Datura. Other wild nightshade species. The presence of these plants prevents a complete interruption of the pest cycle between tomato seasons. 5.11.12 Delayed quarantine and monitoring procedures When the pest appeared in new areas, many producers or inspectors were not sufficiently aware of its symptoms. This led to: Diagnosis delayed. Continued circulation of infected fruits. Transferring seedlings between regions. Do not install traps early. Failure to dispose of field waste. The focus expanded before it was officially announced. Once the pest spreads over a large area, complete eradication becomes very difficult. 5.11.13 Pesticide resistance Pesticide resistance does not represent a geographic route of transmission for the insect, but it does increase its ability to establish after arrival. When an insect community with degrees of resistance enters an area that relies on the same pesticides, initial treatments may fail to eliminate it. Intensive spraying after discovering the pest also leads to: Electing more resistant individuals. Kill local bio enemies. Increased cost of control. The presence of foci capable of spreading. Therefore, imposing chemical spraying alone is not sufficient to prevent the expansion of the pest.

The Tuta Absoluta movement can be divided into two levels: Distribution is short and medium distance It is mainly caused by moths, and is transmitted between: Plants. Adjacent fields. Villages. Nearby production areas. Long-distance propagation Mainly associated with human activity, especially: International trade. Transporting fruits. Transplanting seedlings. Movement of packages. Crop trucks. Wholesale markets. Packing stations. The combination of the two patterns explains the great speed of the global invasion; Trade moves the pest to a distant focus, and then adult moths and local crop movements spread it into the new area.

sectionTitle: 5.13 Why is it difficult to eradicate after stabilization?
text: A small focus can be eradicated if detected early and isolated, but eradication becomes difficult after the pest has reached several farms. This is due to: Multiple phases present at the same time. The larvae hide inside the plant. The presence of pupae in soil and waste. Butterflies moving between fields. Overlapping lugs. Trade fruits daily. The presence of alternative families. Difficulty coordinating control among all farmers. The ability of a small number of individuals to establish a new society. Resistance of some populations to pesticides. When the pest becomes widespread, the goal of eradication shifts to: Prevent increased density. Minimize damage. Protection of fruits. Reducing sources of infestation. Slow the development of resistance. Preventing its movement to new areas.

Monitoring should precede the appearance of damage When fruit damage appears on a large scale, the pest is already established. Monitoring should therefore begin with traps and plant inspection before losses appear. The nursery is part of the protection system An infected seedling is able to transmit the pest to a new field, even if it is far from the nearest known outpost. Waste management is a regional procedure Leaving an infected field after harvest does not only harm its owner, but also turns it into a source of butterflies that attack the surrounding fields. Administrative boundaries do not stop the pest Fruits and butterflies move between centers, governorates, and countries, and therefore management requires coordination between producers and regulatory authorities. The correct name is necessary Confusion between Tuta and Leaf Miner delays diagnosis and leads to the use of inappropriate monitoring and control methods. Spraying alone does not prevent spread The history of the invasion has proven that reliance on pesticides did not stop the expansion of the pest, but rather led in many areas to the emergence of resistance and damage to biological enemies.

Tuta absoluta originated in South America, was scientifically described from Peru in 1917, and then became a major pest of tomatoes in a large number of countries on the continent. The global invasion phase began when it was discovered in Spain at the end of 2006. It then spread rapidly throughout southern Europe and the Mediterranean basin. It arrived in North Africa in 2008, where it was recorded in Morocco, Algeria, and Tunisia, then appeared in Egypt in 2009. Egyptian studies indicate that the first record occurred in Marsa Matruh, before it spread during the following years in most Egyptian tomato-producing areas. In the same year, the pest was recorded in Türkiye, from where its spread spread to the Levant, Iraq, Iran, and the Arabian Peninsula, and then to South, Central, and East Asia. Within about ten years of its entry into Africa, it reached all the main regions of the continent, and it also continued to expand within India, Nepal, Pakistan, Central Asia, and China. This spread did not result from a single factor, but rather from an interaction: International and regional trade. Transporting fruits, seedlings and containers. Natural flight of butterflies. Overlapping tomato growing seasons. The presence of alternative families. Larvae hide inside tissues. Multiple generations. Suitability of climatic conditions. Delay in detection and diagnosis. Difficulty in eradication after the formation of several foci. This trip demonstrates that management of Tuta absoluta does not begin with spraying the infected field, but rather begins with preventing its transmission, examining seedlings and fruits, setting traps early, removing crop residues, and coordinating control between fields and regions. Morphological Description Morphological description means the study of the phenotypic and structural characteristics that characterize an organism, such as shape, size, color, body division, appendages, wings, bristles, and diagnostic signs. Morphological description represents an important basis for identifying Tuta absoluta and distinguishing it from other pests of tomato crops, especially species that may cause similar symptoms, such as leafminer flies, potato tuber moth, tomato pinworm, and tomato fruitworm. Tuta absoluta undergoes a complete metamorphosis, which includes four clear stages that differ in form and function: The egg. Larva. Pupa. The adult insect or moth. The transition between these stages is not merely a gradual increase in size; each stage differs in body structure, behavior, location on the plant, and role within the life cycle. The egg represents the beginning of the new individual, the larva is the stage of feeding and causing damage, the pupa is the stage of rebuilding the body and transformation, while the adult moth represents the stage of reproduction, spreading, and laying eggs.

sectionTitle: 6.2 The importance of morphological description in identifying the pest
text: Finding a mine inside a tomato leaf is not enough to confirm the presence of Tuta absoluta because a number of insects can form mines inside the leaves. The presence of a larvae inside a tomato is not sufficient to rule that it is a tomato larvae, because the fruits may be infected with other types of larvae. The correct diagnosis depends on combining a number of pieces of evidence, including: The shape of the egg and where it is placed. Larva shape and color. Having a clear head and legs. Marks on the back of the head. Number of larval instars. The shape of the mine and frass inside it. The position of the pupa and the nature of its surrounding covering or cocoon. Adult moth morphology. The color of the wings and the markings on them. The shape of the antennae. Insect size. Results of pheromone traps. Microscopic examination when needed. The level of certainty increases whenever several compatible characteristics come together.

Tuta absoluta belongs to fully metamorphosed insects, and this type of development is known as: Complete Metamorphosis or Holometabolous Development In it, the larvae differ clearly from the adult insect. The larva: Its body is worm-shaped. It has biting mouthparts. They feed on plant tissue. They move inside mines and between plant parts. Represents the phase responsible for agricultural damage. As for the whole insect: It is a small winged moth. It does not create mines or holes inside the tissue. They mate and lay eggs. It spreads the infestation between plants and fields. The two stages are separated by the pupal stage, during which profound internal and external changes occur that lead to the formation of the adult moth.

sectionTitle: 6.4 Morphological change during the life cycle
text: The insect begins as a very small egg that is often deposited individually on plant parts. After the completion of embryonic development, a tiny larva emerges from it and begins searching for a suitable location to penetrate the plant tissue. The larvae goes through four larval stages: First larval instar L1. Second larval instar L2. Third larval instar L3. Fourth larval instar L4. Successive larval instars are separated by molts, during which the larva sheds its old outer covering so the body can increase in size. The larva does not only change in size, but changes occur in: Head width. Body length. Body color. Clarity of the annular division. The amount of fabric consumed. Mine size. The ability of the larvae to move between plant parts. Clarity of diagnostic signs. After completing the fourth larval instar, the larva stops feeding, enters the prepupal stage, and then becomes a pupa. After completion of metamorphosis, a small moth emerges from the pupa, completely different from the larva in body shape, wings, mouthparts, and biological function.

Morphological description must be separated from biological description. Morphological description Addresses: Figure. Color. Measurements. Body parts. Virtual signs. wings. Legs. Antennae. Diagnostic features. Bio description Addresses: Duration of each phase. Growth rate. Behavior. Fertility. Feeding places. Response to heat and humidity. Activity timing. Number of generations. The two areas may be related to each other, but the length of the egg or the color of the larvae is a morphological characteristic, while the duration of egg incubation or the speed of the larvae's growth is a biological characteristic.

The color of Tuta absoluta instars is not completely constant, but rather changes depending on several factors, the most important of which are: Phase age. Degree of development. Near moulting time. Near hatching or moth emergence. Type of food. Environmental conditions. How to store the sample. Lighting used during the examination. The new egg differs in color from the egg that is close to hatching, the newly hatched larva is different from the fully developed larva, and the new pupa differs from the pupa from which the moth is close to emerging. This is why color is not relied upon alone in diagnosis, but rather color is linked to size, shape, where the phase is found, and other anatomical signs. Published measurements also differ to limited degrees between studies, as a result of differences in: Geographic breeds. Temperatures. Plant host. Measurement methods. The state of the specimen is alive or preserved. The exact age within the instar. When presenting measurements, an approximate range is used to express natural variation, rather than treating a single number as a fixed limit for all individuals.

Many characteristics of Tuta absoluta can be initially identified in the field using: Hand magnifying glass. white paper. Precision brush. Soft tweezers. Collection bags or containers. Pheromone traps. Phone camera with zoom lens. But some precise characteristics cannot be confirmed with the naked eye, especially: Details of the egg's surface. Head capsule measurements. Arrangement of larval hairs. Veining of the wings. Characteristics of the reproductive organs. Differences between closely related species. These characteristics require a dissecting microscope, and final confirmation in difficult cases may require molecular analysis.

Different phases need appropriate collection methods. Eggs Cut a small piece of paper around the egg instead of trying to remove it directly because slight pressure may cause it to shatter. Larvae The mine is opened gradually using a needle or fine forceps, avoiding pressure on the larva's body. Pupae It is collected along with part of the leaf, cocoon, or surrounding soil, as violent removal of the covering may damage the pupa. Whole insects They are collected from traps or plants, while preserving the wings and scales, because intense friction leads to the scales falling off and changing the external appearance. Sample information must be recorded, including: Collection date. Location. Plant host. The affected plant part. existing phase. Damage form. Type of trap when collecting moths.

sectionTitle: 6.9 The difference between field diagnosis and taxonomic confirmation
text: The agricultural engineer can diagnose the infestation in the field to a good degree when the distinctive signs are met, such as: Irregular blotch-like mines. Presence of frass inside the mine. A larva with a pronounced head and legs. The presence of a dark band behind the head in advanced larvae. Infestation of growing tips and fruits. Catching Tuta moths in pheromone traps. But taxonomic confirmation becomes necessary when: The pest appears in an area where it was not registered. There are similar types. The results are used in scientific publishing. An agricultural quarantine study is being conducted. The sample is damaged or incomplete. There are inconsistent results between the trap and the symptoms. In these cases, the diagnosis depends on microscopic or molecular characteristics, and not on the visible damage alone.

sectionTitle: 6.10 Relationship between shape and phase location
text: The Find the Phase website provides a helpful guide, but it is not sufficient on its own. Eggs may be found on leaves or young parts, while larvae are found inside leaves, stems, or fruits. The pupa may form inside a fold in the leaf, inside a silk cocoon, or near the surface of the soil. Adult moths often hide among plants, and males can be detected using pheromone traps. However, these locations may change depending on the circumstances, so a combination of: Al-Tur location. Its shape. Its size. Its color. Diagnostic signs. The nature of the surrounding damage.

Identification proceeds through the life stages from smallest to largest: The egg Their shape, color, size, placement, and changes are examined before hatching. Larva Larval age, color, head capsule, legs, prothoracic shield, body shape and distinguishing characteristics from other leafminers are examined. Pupa Its shape, color, length, position, presence of the silk cocoon, and changes that precede the moth's emergence are examined. The whole insect Body length, wingspan, body color, wings, dark spots, whiskers, antennae, and sex differences are examined.
